# Squabbit - Full Product Knowledge Base

> This is the full product knowledge base used by Squabbits in-app AI assistant. It covers every feature, format, setting, and edge case in Squabbit. AI models and search crawlers can use this as the authoritative reference for what Squabbit does.

Companion to /llms.txt. Source of truth for the in-app AI assistant. Homepage: https://www.squabbitgolf.com

---

# Setup and Lifecycle

## Tournaments, Leagues, and Clubs: The Three Group Types

Squabbit organizes all golf activities around three top-level entities: **Tournament**, **League**, and **Club**. Each is a "group" that holds players, settings, and history, but they serve different purposes and complement each other.

- **Tournament**: A single competition with a defined date (or consecutive days). Use a tournament for a one-off event: a charity scramble, a bachelor-trip skins game, a club championship weekend, or any other self-contained competition. Tournaments contain one or more rounds, one or more scoring formats, a player roster, and produce a live leaderboard.
- **League**: A season-long container that holds multiple tournament events and tracks cumulative standings across all of them. Use a league when players compete every week (or month) and you want season-wide points, averages, or standings. A league's events are individual tournaments linked to it, and the league leaderboard aggregates results from all those events.
- **Club**: An organizational home that can contain both leagues and tournaments in one place. Use a club when a golf club or recurring group wants a single hub for all its activities. A Wednesday ladies league, a Saturday skins league, and the annual club championship can all live under one club. Clubs also serve as a master roster so player data flows from the club into every event without re-entry.

When you tap the **+** button from the home screen you are offered all three types, each with a brief description to help you decide. You can also create a tournament or league from directly inside a club or league, which automatically links the new event to that parent.

## Creating a Tournament

### Starting the Setup Wizard

To create a new tournament, tap the **+** button and select **Tournament**. If you are inside a league or club when you create it, the new tournament is automatically linked to that parent and inherits some of its settings. A setup wizard launches as soon as you tap create.

The wizard collects the fundamental details:

1. **Tournament name**: The name players will see everywhere in the app.
2. **Tournament style**: **Single round** or **Multi-round**. If you pick Multi-round, a follow-up dialog asks for the **Number of rounds** (up to 20). Once a tournament is multi-round, you cannot change it back to single round — only the other way around (single can grow into multi by adding more rounds later).
3. **Course selection**: For multi-round tournaments, you are first asked whether you are playing a **Single course**, **Multiple courses**, or **Not sure**. Choosing Multiple courses (or Not sure) shows an info note explaining that each round needs its own course assigned individually. Choosing Single course (or any single-round tournament) opens the course search.
4. **9-hole course handling**: When the chosen course only has 9 holes, a follow-up dialog asks how you want to play it: **Play 9 holes**, **Play it twice as an 18-hole loop**, or **Combine with another front/back nine** to build an 18-hole layout from two different 9-hole courses.
5. **Multi-nine courses**: When the chosen course has more than 18 holes (27, 36, 45, or 54 holes organized into named nines), a picker asks which nines you are playing and in what order. You can even pick the same nine twice.
6. **Tees**: After picking the course you select which tees players are allowed to play from. If you whitelist only one tee, every player is auto-assigned to it. If you whitelist multiple tees, each player picks their own tee when they open the scorecard.
7. **Format**: The scoring format(s) that determine how the winner is decided.

After the wizard you land on the full tournament settings page, where every detail can be adjusted before (and after) the tournament starts.

### Single-Round vs. Multi-Round Tournaments

**Single round** is for one round of golf played to determine a winner. All players play the same course on the same day and the leaderboard is final after that round.

**Multi-round** is for tournaments that span multiple days, or for days where players play more than one round. In a multi-round tournament each round (called a **tournament round**) is created independently and can have its own course, tees, date, hole setup, and format. For example:

- A four-day Ryder Cup event uses multi-round; each day is one round and the format accumulates over all four.
- A corporate outing that plays a morning Best Ball and an afternoon Stableford uses multi-round with two rounds on the same day, each with its own format.
- A weekend trip where day 1 is Strokeplay and day 2 is Skins uses multi-round with each day assigned its own format.

You can also run single-round formats inside a multi-round tournament. For instance, a multi-round Ryder Cup tournament might have a single-round Best Ball format active only on day 2.

### Per-Round Settings

Each tournament round has its own independent settings:

- **Date and optional end date**: When the round starts. An optional **End date** lets a single round span more than one calendar day, useful for club qualifying that runs across a week or for any event where players are free to post their round anytime within a window.
- **Course and tees**: Each round can use a different course, and multiple courses can be in use within one round (useful for very large shotgun events spanning several facilities)
- **Number of holes**: 18, or 9 holes starting from hole 1 (front) or hole 10 (back), or a custom starting hole. On a multi-nine course (27+ holes), you instead pick which named nines the round plays and in what order. When you switch a round between 18 and 9 holes (or flip Front/Back 9), the app prompts you to migrate any existing players' tee-time **starting holes** so their assigned tee times keep matching the new round configuration.
- **Tee time interval**: The number of minutes between tee times
- **Shotgun start** toggle: For formats where everyone starts simultaneously on different holes
- **Hole competitions**: Per-hole side games like closest to the pin or longest drive, each configurable with its own purse
- **Per-player handicap overrides**: Set a different playing handicap for any specific player for just this round, without changing their overall tournament handicap
- **Scorecard PDF message**: Optional text printed at the top of paper scorecards for this round

### Adding Scoring Formats

A tournament can have multiple formats running simultaneously. For example, Stroke play for the individual leaderboard and a Best Ball for a team leaderboard. You add formats from the tournament settings page; each one gets its own configuration section. Each format supports configuration of handicap percentage applied to the course handicap, purse distribution, tie-break method, max hole score cap, and whether to produce separate gross and net sub-leaderboards when handicaps are enabled.

**Scoping a format to specific rounds.** Every format on a multi-round tournament has a **Rounds** setting that controls which tournament rounds the format applies to. By default a format applies to every round, but you can pick a subset (a single round, or any combination). This lets you use the **same format type more than once in the same tournament**, with each copy scoped to different rounds and configured independently. Common uses:

- **Different skins purse per round/day:** Add two (or more) Skins formats. Scope the first to round 1, the second to round 2, and so on. Each Skins format has its own purse, carry-over setting, and other Skins-specific options.
- **Different format per day:** Day 1 plays Stroke Play (scoped to round 1), day 2 plays a Skins game (scoped to round 2). Both live in the same tournament and their leaderboards appear side by side.
- **A bonus format on one round only:** Add a Best Ball format scoped to only the final round of a 4-round tournament, alongside a Stroke Play format that runs across all 4 rounds.

The pattern is general: anywhere you would otherwise wish for "per round X" of a format setting that isn't itself per-round, add another copy of that format scoped to the round you care about and configure it independently.

### Simulator and Indoor Events

When creating a tournament inside a club that is flagged as a **simulator** club, the tournament automatically inherits the simulator flag. You can also toggle simulator mode on any tournament independently. Simulator tournaments behave similarly to outdoor tournaments but mark themselves clearly in the UI, and some pace-of-play and live-event features are tuned for indoor play.

## The Tournament Edit Page (Admin Settings)

The tournament edit page is where admins control every aspect of how the tournament behaves. It is organized into four tabs:

- **Setup**: Name, start date, start time, the **Formats** list (add, remove, and configure scoring formats, flights, teams, and matches), and the tournament rounds list (course override flow and per-round configuration)
- **Players**: The participant list (add, remove, and manage players)
- **Fees**: Registration fees, payment methods, and custom registration fields
- **Advanced**: Everything else, grouped into expandable sections with a search bar at the top:
  - **General**: Mark complete, name/photo/location/description, simulator flag, currency, sponsors, pace of play (indicator and per-par target minutes), scorecard markers (attesters), official event mode
  - **Player settings**: Self-registration toggles and registration date windows, max players (waitlist), tee time selection, course selection, match substitution by players, scorecard entry permissions (who can enter scores, any-time entry, edits after completion), photo uploads, expenses, side game purses, required stats (putts, GIR, FIR, lost balls), mulligans, player messaging
  - **Layout**: Which tabs and elements are visible to players (leaderboard tab, schedule tab, players on home page, balances, activity tab, activity for spectators)
  - **Leaderboard**: Show handicaps, show avatars, hide results, hide-results-after-hole, show-for-admins-when-hidden, show-when-my-rounds-complete
  - **Notifications**: Per-format match results, closest-to-the-pin and longest drive, hole-out, birdies, registration and unregistration notifications for admins
  - **Web Browser**: Public website and sharing settings, auto-scrolling leaderboard
  - **Actions**: Print scorecards, export as CSV, import rounds, copy tournament, convert to league, transfer ownership (owner only), scorecard recovery (recently deleted items), delete tournament

**Where is the max hole score setting?** Max hole score is a **per-format** setting, not a tournament-wide one: go to the tournament edit page's **Setup tab, open Formats, tap the format name, then tap "Max hole score"**. Because it lives on each format, different formats in the same tournament can score with different caps (for example, stroke play capped at net double bogey while skins counts every stroke). In app versions before 6.6.0 this was a single tournament-wide setting on the edit page; if you set it there in the past, re-set it on each format.

Most changes save immediately as you toggle them; you do not need to tap a save button.

### Pace of Play Settings

Tournaments have a built-in pace-of-play system. From the tournament settings:

- **Show pace of play indicator**: Turns the feature on. When on, the digital scorecard shows whether the group is ahead or behind expected pace, and a pace banner appears on tee time cards.
- **Par 3 target minutes**, **Par 4 target minutes**, **Par 5 target minutes**: How many minutes a group is expected to spend on each hole based on its par. Defaults are reasonable for typical courses; adjust if your course plays faster or slower.

When printing paper scorecards, you can optionally add a "Show pace of play" overlay so target finish times for each hole are printed alongside the hole numbers.

### Tab Visibility Settings

Each tournament has three player-facing tabs that admins can hide for non-admins:

- **Leaderboard tab visible**: When off, players cannot see the leaderboard tab. Useful for in-person events where you want the leaderboard reveal to be controlled.
- **Schedule tab visible**: When off, players cannot see tee times or pairings. Useful for surprise pairing events.
- **Activity tab visible**: When off, the chat/activity feed is hidden from all non-admins.
- **Activity tab visible for spectators**: Lets you hide the activity feed from spectators specifically while keeping it visible to participants.

Admins always see all tabs regardless of these settings.

## Leagues: Season-Long Competition

### What a League Is

A league is a season container. It holds a persistent player roster across the whole season, a set of tournament events, and one or more league formats that aggregate tournament results into season-wide standings.

Think of a league as the "season" and each linked tournament as one "event" in that season. The league leaderboard shows where every player stands across the whole season, not just in the most recent event.

### League Formats: How Standings Are Calculated

A league can have one or more **league formats**. Each format defines a different way to aggregate individual tournament results into season standings:

- **Sum**: Add up all the players' scores or points from each event
- **Average**: Average the scores or points across events played
- **FedEx points**: Assign a ranked points total after each event and accumulate them; the points distribution table is fully customizable
- **Elimination bracket**: Players are eliminated from the league after losing, similar to a playoff structure
- **Individual eclectic**: Each player builds one composite round from their best score on every hole across all the events in the season. For each hole, only the player's single best score (from any event) counts, and the composite total ranks the leaderboard. Works with Strokeplay and Stableford events. On the league leaderboard, tap a player's total to open their composite scorecard showing the best score per hole and which event's date it came from.

League formats can be set up for individual players or for persistent teams. Team league formats track the same teams across all events in the season.

### Counting Existing Events Toward a New League Format

When you add a brand-new league format to a league that already has events, Squabbit pops a **Count existing events** bottom sheet so you don't have to open every past event one by one. The sheet lists every tournament in the league and the formats inside each, with a checkbox per format. All formats are checked by default. Uncheck any tournament format you do not want to feed into the new league format, then tap **Save**. Each checked format gets a "Count towards {new league format}" toggle flipped on automatically.

### Adding Events to a League

Three ways to bring tournament events into a league:

1. **Create from inside the league**: Tap the **+** button on the league's events tab. The tournament is created pre-linked to the league. It inherits the league's admin access list, custom user fields, sponsors, and public website setting. If you want players to be notified that a new event has been posted, you are prompted to send a notification at creation.
2. **Import an existing tournament or a CSV**: From the league settings, tap **Import tournaments** and pick your source:
   - **Existing**: Import a standalone Squabbit tournament you already created. The tournament is linked to the league and standings are recalculated if it was already marked complete.
   - **CSV**: Upload a spreadsheet of past results from a tournament that was played outside Squabbit. The CSV is converted into a tournament shell linked to the league so those scores still count in season standings.

   Clubs have the same Existing-vs-CSV picker plus a third option to **Import an existing league** under the club.
3. **Import a past event**: For historical results from before Squabbit was in use, you can manually enter past event results so they count in the season standings.

### Removing or Moving a Tournament Out of a League

You can take an event back out of a league. Open the event's card menu and choose **Remove from league**. This removes the tournament from the league and pulls its scores out of the league leaderboard, and the event stops showing in the league's events tab. The tournament itself is **not** deleted: all its scores stay intact and you can still find it in the **Tournaments** section of the Competitions tab on the home screen. League standings and stats are recalculated so they no longer reflect that event.

You can also **move a tournament to another league**. The event is removed from its current league and added to the new one; its scores stop counting toward the old league and start counting toward the new one instead. The scores inside the tournament itself never change.

Clubs work differently from leagues here. You can **add** a tournament to a club (from the club's **Import tournaments**), but there is currently **no way to remove a tournament from a club** once it has been added, removing and moving events out is a league-only capability. If you need a tournament out of a club, the only options are to delete the tournament itself or to delete the club.

### New-Event Cards and Declining

When a new event is added to a league, league members see a **"New event added"** card in their feed showing the event with **View** and **Decline** buttons. Admins can also post (or re-post) this card at any time: open the event's **Edit tournament** page and tap **Notify league of event** in the advanced settings. This posts an event card to the league chat so players can register for or decline the event. The action only appears on tournaments that are linked to a league. Tapping **View** opens the event; tapping **Decline** (after a confirmation) flips the card to a **"Declined"** state to tell the organizer you won't be playing this one. The decline is recorded immediately, and the league admins are notified (and it's logged in the admin feed under Roster). Declining is just a heads-up to the organizer; it doesn't remove you from the league.

### League Handicap Types

The league's handicap setting determines what handicap each player uses when they are added to a league event:

- **Current index** (default): Each player's handicap for a given event is set to their current handicap index on the day they are added to that event. The index is pulled from WHS/GHIN if set up, or calculated from Squabbit round history otherwise.
- **Fixed handicap**: Each player's handicap is set when they first join the league and stays constant for every event in the season. An admin can manually adjust individual fixed handicaps at any time. This option is useful when you want to lock handicaps at the start of a season.
- **League events**: Handicaps are calculated purely from rounds played within the league. Players start without a handicap until they complete enough events for a handicap to be calculated. You choose which seasons' data to include in the calculation.

### League Leaderboard Settings

You can customize what the league leaderboard shows:

- **Minimum events to rank**: How many events a player must complete before appearing in the ranked standings (defaults to 1)
- **Show number of events**: Displays how many events each player participated in alongside their season standing
- **Only count completed events**: When on, an event's results only count toward standings once the event is marked complete
- **Show winnings**: Toggle to hide all prize-pool information on the league side
- **Show handicaps in leaderboard**: Reveal each player's handicap as a leaderboard column
- **Handicap cap**: Set a maximum handicap for the whole league; players with a higher index are capped at this number for all league events
- **Leaderboard tab visible** and **activity tab visible** (overall and for spectators): Same tab-hiding controls as tournaments

### Recalculate Leaderboards and Stats

Under the league's **Other** section is a **Recalculate leaderboards and stats** action. Tapping it opens a confirmation dialog warning that the operation is expensive. When confirmed, every leaderboard and every stat in the league is rebuilt from scratch from the underlying tournament rounds.

Use this when an old event's scores were edited retroactively and league standings or league-wide stats look stale. It is not needed for normal play — leaderboards update in real time as scores come in — but it is the right escape hatch when something looks wrong after a manual data fix.

### League Seasons

Each league represents one season. When a season ends, you start a new season using **Create new season** from the league card menu. This opens a dialog where you choose:

- **Copy players**: Bring the player roster from the current season into the new one; their private data (email, phone) copies over too
- **Copy events**: Create new tournament shells based on the current season's events, shifted forward by one year

The new season is a completely independent league with its own standings. The previous season's results stay in the original league and are never modified. When using the League Events handicap type, the new season can optionally include rounds from selected previous seasons when calculating players' starting handicaps.

## Clubs: The Organizational Layer

### What a Club Is

A club provides a home page and a shared identity for a golf club, association, or any recurring group. Members of the club can see all the club's events (tournaments and leagues) in one place, and admins manage everything from one dashboard.

Key things clubs provide:

- **Unified events listing**: All tournaments and leagues are shown in one tab, filterable by open vs. completed, sortable by date
- **Persistent member roster**: The club has its own member list that persists independent of any individual event. Members retain their data (handicap, custom fields, private info) across the whole club lifetime
- **Master-roster sharing**: When adding players to any event hosted by the club, an admin can pull from the club's member roster with a single tap, instead of searching and re-adding players from scratch for every event
- **Private data inheritance**: Linking a tournament or league to a club means that when a player is added to any event, their private information (email, phone number, date of birth) is automatically copied from the club member record. Players only need to provide this data once, at club membership time
- **Home course**: A club can store its home course, available as a default when creating new events
- **Activity feed**: A shared chat/activity stream for club announcements and event updates

### Notify Members for New Events

Both leagues and clubs have a **Notify players for new events** switch (leagues) or **Notify members for new events** switch (clubs) in their settings. When on, every member of the league or club receives a push notification the moment an admin adds a new tournament under that league or club. Turn it off if your group runs frequent events and you do not want to spam members with every announcement.

### Creating Events from a Club

Inside the club's events tab, tap **+** and choose whether to create a new tournament or a new league. Both inherit the club's simulator setting. Creating an event automatically posts a message to the club's activity feed so members see the announcement.

### Club Membership and Self-Join

Club membership works the same way as tournaments and leagues:

- Admins can add members directly, searching by name, importing from a CSV, or pulling from previous events
- The admin can enable **allow self-join** so players can add themselves using the invite code or app link
- Players can be given **admin rights** to help manage the club

### Removing an Event from a Club

To detach a tournament or league from its club, open that event's settings and choose **Remove from club** (confirm when prompted). This unaffiliates the event: it keeps all of its own data (roster, scores, private info already copied in) and simply stops being listed under the club and stops pulling from the club's shared roster.

**Deleting a club does not delete or orphan its events.** When you delete a club, Squabbit first detaches every affiliated tournament and league, so they carry on as standalone events rather than pointing at a club that no longer exists. Nothing inside those events is lost.

## Converting a Tournament Into a League

### When to Convert Instead of Clone

If you set up a tournament with several rounds and later realize the event should actually live as a **league** (so each round can have its own standings, registration, and stats), use **Convert to league** instead of starting over. The new league is created with one event per existing tournament round, and all the scores you've already entered come with it.

### How to Convert

1. Open the tournament's edit page (admin only).
2. Tap **Convert to league**. The tile only appears when the tournament has **2 or more rounds** and is not already linked to a league.
3. A confirmation dialog explains what happens, what is preserved, and what is lost.
4. Tick the **I understand this cannot be undone** checkbox and tap **Convert**.
5. Watch the progress as Squabbit creates the league, copies chat messages, and turns each round into its own event.

### What Gets Preserved

- **Players**, including private data
- **Scores** on every round
- **Courses, dates, and tees**
- **Chat messages, photos, and sponsors** move to the new league

### What Is Lost

- **Fees, purses, and payment records** are not migrated. Re-create them on the new league or its events if you need them.

After conversion the new league opens automatically. The original tournament is replaced by the league, so all bookmarks, deep links, and shared scorecards now point at the new structure.

## Cloning a Tournament

### When Cloning Is Useful

Cloning is the right tool when you run the same event format repeatedly. Rather than configuring every format, flight, fee, and setting from scratch each time, you copy an existing tournament and adjust just the date, name, and any other things that change.

### How to Clone

Open the existing tournament you want to copy, open the tournament settings page (admin only), and tap **Copy tournament**. A bottom sheet appears with:

- **New name**: Pre-filled with the original name plus "(copy)"; edit freely
- **New date**: Pre-filled with one week after the original date; tap to pick any other date
- **Course override**: Only appears if the original tournament uses the same single course across all rounds. Lets you swap to a different course for the copy. If you change the course, you must also select tees before the copy can be created.
- **Copy players** checkbox: See "With or without players" below

### What the Clone Copies

The copy includes:

- All **formats** with full configuration: scoring method, handicap percentage, purse structure, tie-break method, hole assignments, flight definitions, and team definitions
- **Registration settings**: The self-add toggle, all registration fields, and date windows. Registration date windows shift automatically: if the original had a registration window that opened 14 days before the tournament date, the copy's window opens 14 days before the new date
- **General settings**: Leaderboard visibility, result-hiding settings, notification settings, custom user fields, sponsors, pace-of-play settings, tab visibility, and scorecard marker settings
- The **league ID** and **club ID** (copied from the original, or you can specify different ones)
- **Pace-of-play** settings (par 3/4/5 target times)

### What the Clone Resets

These are always reset in the copy:

- **Photos**: Replaced with a stock photo; user-uploaded cover photos are not copied
- **Player list**: See "With or without players" below
- **Registrations**: No one is marked as registered in the new tournament
- **Completed status**: The copy is always not completed
- **Per-player extra points**: Manual bonus points on individual formats are cleared (these manual adjustments are scoped to each tournament, not carried forward)
- **Manual format winners**: Any manually assigned flight or overall winners are cleared
- **Invite code**: A fresh unique code is generated so the old code cannot be accidentally used to join the copy
- **Public visibility**: Always starts private

### With or Without Players

**Copy with players** includes the entire player roster. Each player is re-added to the new tournament so their profile is correctly linked and all team and flight assignments are preserved. Private data (email, phone) is re-copied from the source tournament's private records.

**Copy without players** creates an empty roster. Teams and flights are also cleared (since they would reference non-existent players). You get format shells and all settings but start filling the player list fresh.

## Admin and Owner Roles

### The Difference Between Owner and Admin

Every group (tournament, league, or club) has exactly one **owner** plus zero or more **admins**.

- The **owner** is the user who created the group. They have all admin privileges plus the ability to transfer ownership and delete the group.
- **Admins** can do almost everything the owner can: edit settings, add and remove players, manage formats, register players manually, see private data, send notifications, mark winners. Admins cannot transfer ownership.
- A normal **participant** sees the player-facing parts of the group but cannot change any admin settings.

### Adding Another Admin

Only an existing admin can promote a participant to admin:

1. Open the **Players** tab.
2. Tap the player you want to promote.
3. Tap **Make admin**.
4. Confirm.

To remove someone's admin rights, tap that admin in the player list and tap **Remove admin rights**. The owner's admin rights cannot be removed (they always have them).

When you make someone an admin of a league, they are automatically also added as an admin to every existing tournament that belongs to that league. Removing league admin rights also removes them from every league event.

### Transferring Ownership

Only the current owner can transfer ownership. From the group settings, find **Transfer ownership**, choose the new owner from your contacts or by searching, and confirm. After the transfer, you become a regular admin (still on the admin list) and the new person becomes the owner.

## Registration, Invite Codes, and Sharing

### Enabling Self-Registration

By default only admins can add players. To open registration to players themselves:

1. Open the tournament or league settings.
2. Under the **Players** section, turn on **Allow self-registration**.
3. A **Register** button becomes visible to all users who open the event.

Once self-registration is on, you can optionally configure:

- **Registration start date**: A date/time before which the Register button is shown as inactive
- **Registration end date**: A date/time after which registration closes
- **Allow self-unregistration**: Players can remove themselves; pair with an **unregistration end date** to lock it after a certain point
- **Allow registration edits after submitting**: Whether players can go back and change their form answers, fee selections, or game opt-ins after initially registering

For league tournaments specifically, you can also toggle **Allow guest registration**. When off, only players who are already in the parent league can register for the event.

### Group Registration

When **Allow group registration** is enabled and a player registers, they can add other players to register alongside them in the same session. Useful for couples, families, or regular playing partners. All players in the session are linked together under a shared registration ID, so the admin can view who registered together. Group discounts on fees (see below) require this to be on.

For team formats, if **Allow team registration** is also enabled, players can form or join a team during the registration flow.

### The Invite Code

Every group (tournament, league, or club) gets a permanent **invite code** on creation. It is a short alphanumeric string. To share it:

- Open the group and tap the share or invite button.
- Tap the invite code button to copy it to your clipboard.
- Players enter this code in the Squabbit app (from the "Join with code" option) to navigate directly to the group.

The invite code works regardless of whether self-registration is on or off. It navigates the player to the group page, but they can only join if self-registration is enabled or an admin adds them.

### App Links, QR Codes, and Spectator Links

The full invite dialog provides multiple sharing options:

- **App link**: A deep link URL that opens directly to the group in the Squabbit app. Copy it and share via text, email, social media, or any messaging platform.
- **QR code**: A scannable QR code that encodes the app link. Displayed in-app, perfect for printing or showing at check-in.
- **Spectator / website link**: A URL to the public web leaderboard. Anyone can view this in a browser without a Squabbit account. Copying or sharing this link also automatically enables the group's public website feature if it was not already on.

### The Registration Form

When players self-register, they fill out a form. The form always includes **name** (required). Additional built-in fields you can add:

- **Email**: Validated for proper format
- **Phone number**
- **Date of birth**
- **Gender**
- **Transport**: walking, cart, etc.

Each field can be marked required or optional. Fields can be reordered and removed (except name). Custom fields also appear in the registration form once added.

## Group Privacy: Who Sees Player Data on the Home Page

Every tournament, league, and club has two privacy controls that decide how much of the player roster is visible to people viewing the group:

### Players (or Members) Visible Switch

A top-level switch labeled **Players visible** (in a club it reads **Members visible**) is on the group settings page. When on, the home page exposes the participant list to anyone who can see the group, including the public spectator website. When off, the player list is hidden from non-admins entirely.

### Field-Level Visibility Dialog

Even when the player list is visible, admins control **which columns** appear publicly. From the players list, tap the overflow menu and choose the visibility dialog. A checklist appears with every column that can be hidden or shown:

- Handicap
- WHS ID
- Email
- Phone number
- Date of birth
- Gender
- Transport
- Tee
- Fee
- Total fees
- Paid
- Won
- Paid out
- Owes

**Name** is always shown and is not toggleable. Check the columns you want public; uncheck the ones you want kept private. The choice is saved on the group and applied to everyone except admins (who always see the full roster).

This is the right tool when you want, say, handicaps and tees visible to the field but email and phone kept private — a common request from organizers worried about sharing contact info publicly.

## Custom Registration Fields

Admins can define custom data fields on any tournament, league, or club to collect information that goes beyond the built-in fields. These appear in the registration form and in the admin's player roster as data columns.

### The Five Field Types

- **Text**: Free-form text entry for things like "T-shirt size," "Home club," or "Emergency contact"
- **Number**: Numeric entry with basic format validation
- **Date**: A date picker
- **Checkbox**: A yes/no toggle for things like "I agree to the terms and conditions" or "Attending the dinner"
- **Choice**: A dropdown list with options you define (e.g., "Meal preference: Chicken / Fish / Vegetarian / Vegan")

### Setting Up Custom Fields

Go to **Registration fields** in the tournament, league, or club settings. Tap **Add field**, select the type, give it a label, and for Choice fields enter the available options. Mark each field required or optional as needed.

When a tournament is created inside a league, it automatically inherits the league's custom fields. When linked to a club, the tournament also inherits the club's custom fields.

### Where Custom Field Data Appears

Once defined, custom fields show up:

- In the **registration form** players fill out when they sign up
- As toggleable **columns in the admin's player roster** (you can show or hide individual columns)
- In the public **home page member fields**, if the admin chooses to make any of them visible there

## Payments, Fees, Expenses, and Balances

Squabbit has a full money-management system for tournaments, leagues, and clubs: collect registration fees, accept payments by credit card or peer-to-peer apps, split shared expenses, track who owes what, and settle everyone up at the end. It all lives on the **Money** tab of the group settings page. (When opened as its own page the screen is titled *Fees and payments*, but the tab a user taps to get there is labelled **Money**. Always tell users to open the **Money** tab, not a "Fees tab".)

### The Money Tab: Setup, Summary, and Balances

The Money tab is pinned with three chips at the top:

- **Setup**: Where admins configure payment methods, create fees, set the payout mode, and toggle expense permissions. This is the configuration screen.
- **Summary** (admin-facing): A financial dashboard card showing the whole group's money picture at a glance. See "The Balances Summary Card."
- **Balances**: A searchable, sortable list of every participant with their current balance. Tap a player to open their full registration receipt and settle up. See "Balances and Per-Player Receipts."

The tab opens on **Setup** by default.

### Payment Methods

Squabbit supports two kinds of payment methods, set up from the **Setup** chip:

- **Auto verified (Stripe / credit card)**: When a player pays by credit card, the money is processed through **Stripe** and their balance updates instantly with no manual confirmation. This is the only auto-verified method. Add it by tapping **Set up payments** and completing Stripe's account onboarding in your browser; Squabbit returns with the account linked. You can disconnect or reconnect Stripe at any time.
- **Manual verification (peer-to-peer handles)**: Handles for paying outside Squabbit, such as **Venmo**, **Zelle**, **PayPal**, **Interac**, **PayID**, or a custom **Other** provider (e.g. Cash App). For these, add a username, phone number, payment link, and/or a QR code image. Squabbit cannot see the money move, so whoever gets paid confirms they received it before a balance updates (the claim/confirm flow described below).

Players also save their own personal payment methods on their profile (**Profile settings → Payment methods**) so that when peer-to-peer settlement happens, others know how to pay them. If a group settles up directly between players, Squabbit prompts a player to add a payment method to their profile so they can be paid.

If no payment method has been set up on the group, fees are still shown but are informational only until a method is connected.

### Creating Fees

You can add multiple fees to a tournament or league. Common examples: an entry fee, a cart fee, a dinner fee, and a mulligan fee. Each fee has:

- **Name**: Shown to players in the registration flow and on their receipt
- **Amount**: Set in the group's currency
- **Optional vs. required**: Required fees must be paid to complete registration; optional fees can be skipped during registration
- **Multi-purchase**: For add-ons like mulligans, set a maximum number of times a player can buy the fee in one registration. When the max is more than 1 the fee is treated as optional automatically.
- **Group discounts**: Define discount tiers based on the number of players registering in a group. For example, "Group of 4: $200 total" effectively gives each of the 4 players a discount. Applies only when group registration is enabled.
- **Description**: Optional explanatory text shown to players

A special **mulligan fee** type is available that integrates with scorecard tracking so mulligan usage is recorded per hole.

### Mulligans: Fixed vs Purchasable

The tournament edit page has a **Mulligans** tile that controls how mulligans are distributed:

- **Fixed**: Every player automatically gets the same number of free mulligans (e.g., "2 mulligans per player"). No purchase, no fee.
- **Purchasable**: Players can buy up to a configured maximum during registration (e.g., "Up to 3 mulligans purchasable"). The mulligan fee on the registration form charges per mulligan.

The Mulligans tile subtitle tells you which mode is active. Tap it to switch modes or change the per-player count.

### Fee Breakdown for Players

When a player pays by credit card, they see a full breakdown before confirming:

- The **Subtotal** (the base fees you configured)
- The **Processing fees** (a percentage of the transaction plus a small fixed amount per transaction); tap the info icon for an explanation
- A **Squabbit service fee** (a percentage set by Squabbit that may vary; this is shown only when it is greater than zero)
- The **Total with fees** the player pays vs. the total you will receive

Whether processing fees are passed to the player on top of your fee, or deducted from what you receive, is a setting in your Stripe configuration. When the player covers them, the **Register** button shows the full amount including fees (e.g. "Register · $54"). Peer-to-peer methods (Venmo, Zelle, etc.) carry no processing fee.

The registration total shown in the headline before checkout includes the required registration fees plus any **non-optional side-game purse buy-ins** (for example a mandatory skins buy-in). Optional fees, shared expenses, and scorecard side games are added to a player's balance later as they occur.

### Require Payment to Register

When **Require payment to register** is on, players cannot complete registration without successfully paying the required fees. With it off, players can register on the honor system and pay later (or never), which works for clubs collecting cash on-site.

### Handling Incomplete Checkouts

If a player starts the Stripe checkout flow but the app closes before the payment completes (e.g., they switch apps), their registration is held in a **pending** state. The next time they open the tournament, Squabbit checks the payment backend to see if the payment actually went through and either finalizes the registration or clears the pending flag automatically.

### Choosing How to Pay

When a player owes money and taps to pay, a **How do you want to pay?** sheet lists the options the group has set up:

- **Credit card** (if Stripe is connected): Routes through Stripe checkout; the balance clears automatically the moment the charge succeeds.
- **Pay via Venmo / Zelle / PayPal / etc.** (any peer-to-peer method on the recipient's profile): Shows the recipient's handle and QR code so the payer can send the money in that app, then tap an **I've paid** button. This records a **claim**: the recipient is notified and confirms they received it, at which point the balance clears.

### Balances and Per-Player Receipts

Every participant has a **registration receipt** that shows their full financial picture in the group. Open it from the **Balances** chip (tap any player) or, for the player themselves, from the **View Registration** button. The receipt lists:

- **Fees**: Their registration fees and any purse buy-ins, each as a line item.
- **Expenses**: Shared expenses they're part of, with the signed amount they fronted or owe.
- **Receive / Pay / Settlement**: A breakdown of who they owe and who owes them, with action buttons.
- **Payments** and **Paid out**: A ledger of payments they've made (entry fees, settling up) and payouts made to them (winnings, prize money). Tap the info icon on either for an explanation. Each payment row shows its **method** (for example "Stripe payment" or "Venmo payment") and, for Stripe charges, the **transaction ID**, which you can tap to copy to your clipboard for reconciling against your Stripe dashboard. Admins can tap a payment row to open it for editing (unless it's been refunded).
- **Total** / **Remaining balance**: The bottom-line number. Positive means they still owe; negative means they're owed.

The **View Registration** button on the registration screen previews the player's own net: **You owe $X** or **You're owed $X**.

The Balances list is searchable and can be sorted (e.g. **Owes most / least**); the sort choice is remembered.

### Settling Up: Pay, Receive, Request, and Mark as Paid

On a balance row, each person sees actions depending on whether they owe or are owed:

- **Pay**: If you owe, pay the other party (or the host). Opens the How-do-you-want-to-pay sheet.
- **Receive**: If you're owed, this section shows who still has to pay you.
- **Request payment**: As the creditor, send a notification asking the other person to pay you (with an optional note).
- **Mark as paid**: As the creditor, clear the balance directly because the other person already paid you outside the app.

In-flight payments show a soft **pending pill** (e.g. "Awaiting confirmation" or "Request sent"). If you're the creditor, tapping your own pending pill opens a **Mark as paid?** confirm so you can settle it on the spot. Both parties see the pending state on their devices; once confirmed, a green **Paid** badge appears.

Admins can also record payments manually. On the **Payments** or **Paid out** section of a player's receipt, an admin can tap **+ Add payment** to log a cash payment received or a payout made, edit an existing payment's amount/date/note, refund a Stripe payment (refunds happen in the Stripe dashboard and sync back automatically), or **unmark as paid** to reverse a recorded payment and put the balance back.

### Payout Mode: Route Through Host vs. Simplify Debts

The **Setup** chip has a **Payout mode** picker that controls how settlements are routed:

- **Route through host**: All payments go from players to the host, and all payouts go from the host to players. Example: Bob owes $20 in entry fees and Carl wins a $20 purse. Bob pays the host $20, then the host pays Carl $20.
- **Simplify debts**: Combines debts and allows direct player-to-player payments to reduce the number of transactions. In the same example, Bob just pays Carl $20 directly: one payment instead of two.

### Shared Expenses (Splitting Costs)

Beyond registration fees, groups can track **shared expenses** like food, lodging, transport, or course fees and split them among the group. Expenses live on the Money tab.

To add one, tap **+ Add expense** and fill in:

- **Title** and optional **description**
- **Amount** and **category** (Food, Lodging, Transport, Course fees, or Other)
- **Date** and an optional **receipt photo**
- **Paid by**: Which member fronted the cost
- **Split**: **Equal** (divides the total evenly across selected participants) or **Custom** (set each person's share by hand)
- **Participants**: Who shares the cost

Each person's expense share flows into their receipt and into settlement: someone who fronted more than their share is owed the difference; someone who fronted nothing owes their share. Tapping an expense shows who paid and how it was split.

By default only admins add expenses. Turn on **Add expenses** in the Money tab's **Setup** settings to let any player add a shared expense; players can only edit or delete expenses they created themselves, while admins can manage any expense.

### How a Purse Works

Every purse in Squabbit — whether it's attached to a scoring format (skins, a low-net purse, etc.) or to a hole competition (closest to the pin, longest drive) — works the same way: it is funded by a per-player **buy-in**, not a flat lump sum. The prize pool is the buy-in multiplied by the number of participants, so a $5 buy-in with 12 players makes a $60 pool. A hole competition's purse uses this identical model — its "purse" is a per-player buy-in too.

Each purse also has:
- A **distribution**, which decides how the pool is paid out. **Percentage for place** gives each finishing place a share of the pool (e.g. 1st 60%, 2nd 30%, 3rd 10%). **Amount for place** pays a fixed dollar amount per place. **Split by points** divides the pool by total points scored (used for skins-style games, where each skin is worth an equal cut).
- **Allow opt-out**: when on, a player can choose not to buy into that purse (they're then excluded from both the buy-in and the winnings).
- **Void if no scores**: when on, if nobody wins (e.g. no one records a qualifying score), the buy-in is never charged and nothing is paid out.

You set all of this up where you configure the format or hole competition in the tournament settings.

### Two Kinds of Game Purse, Two Places to Settle

Purse winnings from games (skins, closest-to-the-pin, matchplay, nassau, any format with a purse) can settle in one of two places, depending on how the purse was set up. This is the key thing to get right when someone asks "how do I mark a game/purse as paid" - the answer depends on which kind it is:

- **Tournament-format purses** (a purse attached to an official tournament format, or a hole competition's purse, set up by the admin in the tournament settings). The buy-ins are collected by the group treasury and the winnings are paid out by the host, so these settle through the **Money** tab → **Balances**, alongside fees and expenses. A winner's prize shows on their per-player receipt under Paid out; an admin records the payout from Balances (tap the player, then **Mark as paid** / **+ Add payment** on their receipt).
- **Side-game purses** (a game added to a scorecard - e.g. a scorecard skins or nassau bet - that the host never holds). These are zero-sum bets between the players, so they settle **directly between players on the scorecard**, NOT through the Money tab. Open the scorecard's payouts view and switch to the **Summary** tab: each settlement row has the same **Pay / Receive / Request / Mark as paid** buttons and pending pills as the Balances page.

If you are unsure which kind the user has, briefly cover both: purse money set up on a tournament format settles in the **Money** tab → **Balances**; a side game added to a scorecard settles on that scorecard's **Payouts → Summary**.

### Settling Side-Game Bets from the Scorecard

Side-game pots (skins, matchplay, nassau) are zero-sum bets between players that the host never holds, so they settle **directly between players on the scorecard**, not through the group balances. Open the scorecard's payouts view and switch to the **Summary** tab: each settlement row has the same **Pay / Receive / Request / Mark as paid** buttons and pending pills as the Balances page.

This works for both tournament rounds and casual social rounds (any scorecard with a side game). A confirmed side-game payment shows a green **Paid** badge on both players' devices. Guests (players who scanned a QR scorecard without an account) can see the pending state but must sign up before they can act; once they sign up their player links automatically and they get the notifications.

### The Balances Summary Card (admin)

The **Summary** chip shows admins a financial dashboard for the whole group. All numbers are **treasury-only** (what flows through the host), so peer-to-peer expense splits and side bets don't distort them. It shows:

- **Outstanding to collect** (the hero number): How much participants still owe the group.
- **Money in**: **Collected** so far, with a progress meter against the total **Billed**.
- **Money out**: **Winnings to distribute** (purse money credited but not yet paid out) and **Already paid out**.
- **Position**: **Treasury balance** (collected minus paid out), **Owed to players**, and **Projected surplus** (the host's expected take after winnings and expenses settle).
- Participant counts: how many are registered, paid in full, and outstanding.

When collapsed, the card summarizes as "$X collected · $Y distributed."

### Payout-Pending Purses

A format purse or hole-competition that hasn't paid out its winnings yet shows a small amber **Pending** pill before the buy-in amount. Tapping it explains what's still needed. Most often, **the payout happens once the tournament is marked complete**.

## Charity Donations

Tournaments can collect **charity donations** from players and spectators. Donations are separate from registration fees and are processed through **Stripe**, so the tournament must have a connected Stripe payment method before you can use them.

### Setting Up a Charity (Admin)

In the **Money** (Fees) tab there is a **Charities** section. Tap **+ Add charity** to create one. (The button is disabled until you connect a Stripe account.) Each charity has:

- **Name** (required)
- **Description** (optional)
- **Image** (optional banner, positioned above or beside the text)
- **Fundraising goal** (optional; when set, the donation widget shows a progress bar and percentage of goal)

Creating a charity automatically adds a donation widget to the group's home tab. From the charity page you can also tap **View donations** to see the running total, the donation count, and every individual donation.

### Donating (Player or Spectator)

Anyone viewing the group taps **Donate** on the charity card to open the donate sheet. They enter a **donation amount** and **their name**, and can tick **Donate anonymously** to keep their name off the public donor list. Tapping **Donate** opens Stripe Checkout to pay. After a successful payment they see a thank-you message, and the donation appears in the totals.

### Where Donations Show Up

- The **home-tab donation widget** shows the charity name and description, the amount raised, the goal and progress bar (if a goal was set), and a list of recent donors with their amounts (anonymous donors show as "Anonymous donor").
- The admin **View donations** page lists every donation with its name (or an anonymous tag), amount, date, status, and a copyable Stripe transaction ID.

Donations are tournament-only (not leagues or clubs). There is no maximum; the amount just has to be greater than zero.

## Fundraiser Auctions

Tournaments can run a **fundraiser auction** (a silent-auction-style raffle of donated items or experiences) right inside the event. Players and spectators bid from the app, and the money collected settles through the tournament's normal payment rails. Auctions are **tournament-only** (not leagues or clubs).

### Setting Up an Auction (Admin)

In the **Money** (Fees) tab, add an auction and open its **Setup** tab. The auction has a few shared settings plus a list of items:

- **Sealed bids**: off by default. When **off** (a standard silent auction), each item shows the live high bid, bid count, and high bidder, which encourages re-bidding. When **on**, bidders can't see the current high bid, count, or leader; only admins see bids, and the winner is revealed only when the auction closes.
- **End date**: an optional automatic close time. Leave it unset to close the auction manually instead. Setting an end date on an already-ended auction re-opens it and runs it again to the new time.
- **Who can bid**: **Participants and spectators** (default), **Participants only** (signed-in tournament members), or **Spectators only** (anyone who is not a tournament member).
- **Close / reopen**: once the auction is live you can close bidding manually at any time, or reopen a closed one.

If the tournament has no payment method connected, a warning appears with a shortcut to set one up, so winners can pay in the app instead of being chased offline.

### Adding Items

Tap **+ Add item** for each thing up for bid. An item has:

- **Name** (required) and an optional **description**.
- **Photos** (optional): the first photo is the card thumbnail; the rest show in a swipeable carousel on the item page.
- **Sponsor** (optional): attach one of the tournament's sponsors as the item's donor, or create a new sponsor on the spot. Auction sponsors are real tournament sponsors, so they also appear wherever sponsors normally do.
- **Estimated value** (optional): a retail reference shown on the item.
- **Starting bid** (optional): the minimum the first bid must be.
- **Bid increment** (optional): how much each new bid must beat the current high bid by. Left unset, any higher amount is accepted.

Items can be dragged into any order. Once the auction is live you can preview exactly what bidders will see, and each item has a shareable link you can send out.

### Bidding (Player or Spectator)

Anyone allowed to bid opens an item, enters a **bid amount**, their **name** and **email** (and optionally a phone number), and taps **Place bid**. Bids are **pledges**: nobody is charged at bid time. The item page updates live: if you're outbid you see it immediately, and while you hold the high bid the form is replaced by a "you're the high bidder" banner (you can't raise against yourself). Each bid must meet the starting bid and clear the required increment. If an item has an end date, a live countdown ticks down and an **Add to calendar** button drops a reminder at the close time.

### Notifications and Winning

- **Outbid**: when someone tops your bid on an open auction, you get an email and an in-app notification with the new minimum and a **Place bid** button to go again.
- **Won**: when the auction closes, each winner gets an email and an in-app notification telling them what they won, for how much, and how to pay.

### Collecting Payment (Admin)

The auction's **Bids** tab is the admin summary: how much the auction raised in total, and a row per item showing the winner, their contact details, the winning amount, and a **Paid** badge once collected. Tap an item to see its full bid history and the collect / mark-paid controls. A won item becomes a **balance** the winner owes, just like a fee:

- **Account-holder winners** (participants or spectators signed in) can open their own receipt and pay through the tournament's payment method (e.g. Stripe); the organizer can also mark them paid.
- **Winners without an account** never hit the ledger, so the organizer collects from them offline using the contact details on the bid and marks the item paid by hand.

A closed auction shows a banner confirming that winner notifications have gone out (or that they're still in flight), so you never think emails were sent before they actually were.

### The Auction Home Widget

A live auction shows up as a widget on the tournament's Home tab with a countdown to close, so members can jump straight into bidding.

## Roster Management

### Ways an Admin Can Add Players

From the **Players** tab of any tournament, league, or club, admins can add players by any of these methods:

- **Search Squabbit users**: Find anyone with a Squabbit account by name; multi-select is supported so you can add several at once
- **Create a new user**: Add someone who does not have a Squabbit account by entering their name and optional handicap; a placeholder profile is created
- **From a previous tournament**: Choose a tournament you administered and pick players from its roster; private data (email, phone) is automatically copied over
- **From your following list**: Select from golfers you follow on Squabbit
- **From a club's members**: If the event is linked to a club, one tap opens the club's member roster for direct selection; private data is copied automatically
- **CSV import**: Bulk-add players from a spreadsheet

You also choose whether the added players are joining as **Participants**, **Spectators**, or onto the **Waitlist**.

### CSV Import Details

The importer expects a header row followed by one player per row. Column matching is by prefix and is case-insensitive. Player names are matched to existing players case-insensitively too: a row for "jane doe" links to an existing "Jane Doe" in the group (or its parent club/league) instead of creating a duplicate.

**Required columns:** First, Last

**Optional columns:** Email, Phone, Gender, WHS ID, Handicap, Team, Tee, Registered, Transport

If your file has columns that do not match any of the above (for instance, a "Home Club" column), you are asked to either cancel the import or proceed. If you proceed, each unrecognized column is automatically created as a new custom **Text** field on the group, and the imported values populate that field for each player.

To get started quickly, download the blank template CSV from the import screen, fill it in, and upload it. The import is limited to **500 players per file**; for larger rosters, split into multiple files.

The import can also create teams: if the Team column is populated, players sharing the same team name are grouped onto a team in each team format on the tournament.

### Inviting Players Who Don't Have a Squabbit Account

The **Create user** option creates a placeholder player profile inside the group. The placeholder is a real entity that can be assigned to scorecards, teams, flights, and matches, and can be scored just like any registered player. If that player later joins Squabbit using the same email or phone number, an admin can merge the placeholder into the new account, preserving all of the player's recorded scores and stats.

A placeholder player counts against the maxPlayers limit and shows in the roster like any other player; the only difference is the lack of an avatar and the inability to receive in-app notifications.

### Editing Player Profile Fields

Tap any player in the roster to open their dialog. As an admin you can edit:

- **Name** and **handicap**
- **Private data**: Email, phone number, date of birth (only admins and the player themselves can see these)
- **Tee assignment** (which tees they play from)
- **Gender** and **transport** preference
- **Custom fields** you have defined on the group
- **Marker** (the player who attests their score) if marker mode is enabled
- **Notes** visible only to admins

You can also use the same dialog to set per-format **extra points** (a manual scoring adjustment scoped to that one tournament format), set a player as **manual winner** of a format or flight, change their **flight** or **team** assignment, mark them as **registered**, or **remove** them from the tournament.

### Calling or Texting Members From the Home Screen

When the players table on the group's Home tab shows the **Phone number** column and a row has a number on file, tapping that cell opens an action sheet with three options:

- **Call**: Launches the phone dialer with the number filled in.
- **Text**: Opens your default SMS app with the number filled in.
- **Edit**: Lets you change the number (the same prompt you get when the cell was empty).

In **multi-select mode** the toolbar gains a **Text** icon next to the **Email** icon. Tapping it composes a group SMS to every selected member who has a phone number on file, so you can blast a quick "tee times moved up 30 minutes" to the entire field. Members without a number are skipped.

### Marking a Player as Registered Manually

Sometimes a player pays at the door or you simply want to skip the registration form. As an admin you can tap a player and mark them registered directly. This puts them into the registered set as if they had completed the form themselves; it does not create a Stripe charge.

### Removing a Player

Tap the player, scroll to **Remove from tournament**, and confirm. Their scorecards and team/flight assignments are also cleaned up. If the player has paid through Stripe, refunds are handled separately on the Stripe dashboard — Squabbit does not refund automatically.

If a player declines a tournament invitation with the **Prevent adding to this group again** option checked, they are added to a decline list. Any subsequent attempt to add them is blocked. This protects players who want to opt out of being repeatedly added by a particular organizer; the admin sees an error message that the player declined previously.

### Spectators vs. Participants

Each member of a group has a **participation type**: Participant, Spectator, or Waitlist. Participants show on leaderboards and play in formats. Spectators can chat in the group's activity feed, view leaderboards, and view their stats from past events; they do not play in the current event. Waitlist users are queued for promotion as described in the Waitlists section.

You can switch a player's type at any time from their player dialog. Moving a participant to spectator removes them from format scoring but keeps them in the group; moving a spectator to participant adds them to the leaderboard.

## Waitlists

### Setting a Player Limit and Enabling the Waitlist

Any tournament, league, or club can have a **maximum players** limit. Set this number in the group settings. Once the limit is reached, new players cannot be added as regular participants and instead go onto the waitlist.

When a player cap is set, a second toggle appears: **Auto-promote from waitlist**. With it on, whenever a spot opens up (a participant unregisters, or you raise the max players limit), the first player on the waitlist is automatically moved to the players list and notified. Promoted players still need to complete their registration, including any required fees, to lock in the spot. This setting works the same way for tournaments, leagues, and clubs.

### How Waitlist Positions Work

Every player on the waitlist has a **waitlist position**: a number starting at 1. Position 1 is next in line. Positions are assigned in the order players are added to the waitlist. Admins can reorder positions at any time (tap a waitlisted player and select **Change waitlist position**, then enter the desired position).

Waitlisted players appear in a dedicated section of the players list, sorted by position. They are not included in leaderboards or format scoring until promoted. A **Waitlist visible** toggle (a sub-option of **Players visible**) controls whether that waitlist section is shown below the players list on the home page so everyone can see who is waiting for a spot. With it on, a waitlisted player can see their own position in the queue.

### Promoting from the Waitlist

You can always promote **manually**: an admin taps a waitlisted player and selects **Add to tournament** (or **Add to league**). The player is moved to active participant status at their current handicap.

If **Auto-promote from waitlist** is on, you don't have to do this yourself. When a spot opens (a participant unregisters or you raise the max players limit), Squabbit automatically promotes the player at position 1 and sends them a notification to complete their registration. With auto-promote off, no one is moved automatically: instead the first waitlisted player gets a "spot available" notification inviting them to register, and whoever registers first claims the spot.

Admins can also remove a player from the waitlist entirely without adding them to the tournament.

### Self-Registration onto the Waitlist

If self-registration is enabled and a player registers when the tournament is already at capacity, they land on the waitlist automatically. They see a waitlist confirmation rather than a full registration confirmation. Admins are notified of all new registrations including waitlist registrations.

## No-Account Access: Spectating and Scoring as a Guest

### Overview

People can follow and score a tournament without creating an account or downloading the app. There are two ways in:

- **Scan the group's QR code** or open its **invite link** (the app link from the invite dialog). This is the main path: no printed scorecard required.
- **Scan a printed scorecard or cart-sign QR code** (available from the **Print assets** section). This drops them straight onto that specific scorecard.

This replaces the old limitation where score entry required scanning a printed scorecard. Now any participant can enter scores without an account.

### "Are you playing, or just spectating?"

When someone opens a tournament's QR code or invite link without an account, they see a branded loading splash, then a prompt: **"Are you playing in [group name], or just spectating?"** with two choices:

- **I'm playing** ("Claim your spot on the leaderboard as yourself")
- **I'm spectating** ("Follow along without entering scores")

The choice is remembered for that group, so they aren't asked again. This anonymous-access flow is for **tournaments**; leagues and clubs still require a real account.

### Spectating Without an Account

An anonymous spectator can view the leaderboard, see scorecards and hole scores, and chat in the group's activity feed. They cannot enter hole scores or see admin features, and no account is created for them.

### Scoring as a Guest: Claiming Your Identity

When someone chooses **I'm playing** (or opens a scorecard QR), Squabbit captures their name and matches them to the field:

1. They enter their **name** (first and last).
2. Squabbit fuzzy-matches that name against the roster, handling nicknames (e.g. Mike/Michael) and small typos.
3. If one player matches, they see an **"Is this you?"** confirmation showing that player's tile. Multiple matches show a "which player are you?" picker. No match shows a help message explaining how to claim their spot or contact the admin.
4. Once confirmed, that identity is saved on the device and reused next time.

If the matched player already has a Squabbit account, Squabbit tells them it's an existing account and asks them to log in to it instead. If the player has no account, they keep playing as a logged-out guest.

A guest can enter scores when they are on the scorecard, the round is open for entry, and the admin hasn't enabled **Only admins can enter hole scores**. If every player on a scorecard already has an account, a guest is told to log in or ask a playing partner to enter their scores.

### Self-Unregistration

A guest (or any participant) can remove themselves from the event. Doing so demotes them to a **spectator**: they can still watch the leaderboard and chat, but no longer appear as a participant or enter scores.

### Persistent Guest Identity

The claimed identity is saved on the device, so the guest picks up mid-round without re-entering anything. The stored identity survives app restarts on the same device.

### Converting a Guest to a Full Account

A **sign-up banner** appears at the top of the app for guest users. Tapping it launches the standard account creation flow. After sign-up or login, the guest state is cleared and the player continues as a fully registered Squabbit user with access to their round history and other features.

## Substitutes in League Tournaments

### When Substitutes Apply

The substitution feature is available exclusively in **league tournaments** (tournaments linked to a league). It handles the common scenario where a league player is unavailable for a specific event and a replacement player takes their spot for that event only.

### Enabling and Using Substitutions

By default, only admins can add subs. You can optionally enable **Allow player match substitution** in tournament settings, which lets a player initiate their own substitution (only for themselves).

**Admin flow for adding a sub:**

1. Open the Players tab and tap the absent player.
2. Select **Add substitute**.
3. A search list appears showing eligible league members not already in this tournament. Both active league participants and league spectators are eligible (excluding system accounts).
4. Select the substitute player.

**What happens automatically:**

- The original (absent) player's status changes to **Spectator** for this event; they are still visible in the league roster and their league standing continues to be tracked.
- The substitute is added as a **Participant** with their tag set to "Subbing for [original player's name]."
- The substitute is assigned to the original player's **scorecards** for all tournament rounds.
- The substitute is placed into the original player's **matches**, **flights**, and **team rosters** across all formats. This means in Best Ball and other team formats the substitute's strokes flow into the original player's team total automatically.

The substitute shows with an "(S)" indicator in the player list so admins can easily identify them.

### Player Self-Substitution

When **Allow player match substitution** is on in tournament settings, a player who knows they cannot make an event can initiate the substitution themselves. They tap their own profile in the tournament and choose **Add substitute**, then pick from eligible league members. The flow and effects are identical to the admin-initiated version. Players can only sub for themselves, not for someone else.

### How Sub Results Count in the League

In league standings calculations, the substitute's tournament score is attributed to the **absent original player**, not to the substitute. The substitute's own league standing is not affected by the round they played as a sub. This means the absent player still accumulates (or loses) league points based on how their substitute played.

### Removing a Substitute

An admin (or the original absent player) can undo a substitution at any time:

1. Tap the substitute player in the player list.
2. Select **Remove substitute**.

This reverses all changes: the original player is restored to active participant status, the substitute is removed from the tournament, and all scorecard, match, and flight assignments are reverted.

## Ghost Players

### What Ghosts Are

A ghost player is an anonymous placeholder used to fill an empty slot in a match or team. Ghosts are not user accounts. They have no profile page, no scorecard, and no round history. They exist only within the tournament's match data.

### Ghost Scoring Rules

Scoring-wise, a ghost plays to **net par on every hole**: the ghost's gross score on each hole equals that hole's par, the ghost receives zero handicap strokes, so the net score is also par. In match play this gives the real player's opponent a fair result rather than a walkover.

### Linked Ghosts (League Tournaments)

When you add a ghost inside a league tournament, you are asked who the ghost is filling in for:

- **Link to an absent player**: Pick a registered league participant who will not be playing. That player moves to Spectator status in this event. The ghost automatically joins all teams that the absent player belongs to, so it appears correctly in every match and team picker. In the league leaderboard, the ghost's round result is attributed back to the absent player (similar to the substitute system but without a real replacement person playing).
- **No one (unlinked)**: The ghost fills an uneven slot with no attribution. It scores to net par but nothing carries back to any league standing.

Outside of league tournaments, ghosts are always unlinked. The same player cannot be both subbed by a real player and represented by a linked ghost — pick one approach for that player.

### Adding and Removing Ghosts

Only tournament admins can manage ghosts. To add a ghost, open the match builder or team manager and use the **Add ghost** option. To remove a ghost, tap it in the match or team view and select **Remove**. When a linked ghost is removed, the original player is automatically restored to Participant status.

## Soft Delete and Recovery (Recently Deleted)

### How Deletion Works

When you delete a tournament, league, tournament round, scorecard, or round in Squabbit, the item is not immediately destroyed. It is moved to a **Recently Deleted** holding area where it stays for **30 days** before being permanently removed. During that window an admin can restore it back to its original state.

### Where to Find Recently Deleted Items

Several entry points open the Recently Deleted view, each scoped to the right type of data:

- **Groups page overflow menu**: Lists deleted tournaments and leagues you owned or administered, on two tabs. Use this to recover an entire deleted tournament or league.
- **Play page overflow menu**: Lists your deleted personal social rounds and scorecards (rounds that are not part of any tournament).
- **Tournament settings page**: A dedicated **Scorecard recovery** tile inside the tournament's edit screen (Other section). Opens the deleted-items view filtered to just this tournament — so you only see deleted tournament rounds, scorecards, and individual player rounds that belong here. Useful when a marker accidentally cleared a scorecard or an admin deleted the wrong round.
- **League settings page**: A dedicated **Event recovery** tile inside the league's edit screen. Opens the deleted-items view filtered to this league — listing tournaments that were deleted from inside this league so you can bring them back without scrolling through every group in your account.

### Restoring an Item

Tap the item in the Recently Deleted list and choose **Restore**. The item returns to where it lived before deletion, with the same name, settings, scores, and so on.

If a tournament had a deleted parent league, the tournament does not appear in the groups Recently Deleted list on its own; restoring the league brings its tournaments back. This prevents you from orphaning a tournament under a still-deleted league.

### Restore Conflicts

When restoring a scorecard or a round, Squabbit checks whether the same player already has a live (non-deleted) round in the same round/tournament slot. If so, it warns you and offers three choices:

- **Keep existing**: Cancel the restore; the live version stays
- **Replace existing**: The live version is moved aside (effectively soft-deleted) and the restored version takes its place
- **Cancel**: Take no action

Carefully pick the one you want — replacing existing data is reversible (the displaced version goes to Recently Deleted), but it can be confusing if multiple people are entering scores at once.

### Permanently Deleting

Each item in Recently Deleted also has a **Permanently delete** action. This skips the 30-day waiting period and removes the data immediately. There is a confirmation prompt because permanent deletes cannot be undone.

After 30 days, items in Recently Deleted are permanently removed automatically.

## Miscellaneous Tournament Settings

### Marking Complete

A tournament can be marked complete by an admin from the settings page. Marking a tournament complete signals to the league that final results should be locked in and counted toward season standings. With **Only count completed events** on at the league level, tournament results contribute to season standings only after this flag flips.

When the tournament is linked to a league and you mark it complete, the confirmation dialog explicitly explains that the tournament's results will be locked in for league standings (assuming the league has "Only count completed events" turned on). This avoids the common surprise of finishing an event but seeing league standings stay stale.
