You can unmark a tournament as complete if you marked it too early. Doing so re-opens it for score entry and edits.

### Scorecard Markers (Attesters)

Some events require players to sign each other's scorecards as attesters. Turn on **Scorecard markers enabled** to require a marker for each scorecard. **Players can choose marker** lets each player pick their own marker; with it off, only admins assign markers.

### Required Stats

You can require players to track specific stats with each hole:

- **Require putts**: Player must enter putt count per hole
- **Require GIRs**: Greens in regulation
- **Require FIRs**: Fairways in regulation
- **Require lost balls**

Stats not required can still be entered optionally by players who want detailed analytics.

### Allow Scorecard Edits After Completion

When on, scores can still be edited after the round is over. Useful for fixing data-entry errors. When off, scorecards lock once submitted and only admins can change them.

### Only Admins Can Edit Hole Scores

A stricter scorecard lock-down: when on, no players can enter scores at all — only admins can. Useful for high-stakes events where the organizer wants full control over score entry.

### Show Pace of Play Indicator

Already discussed under Pace of Play settings above. Toggled in the tournament edit page.

### Allow Side Game Purses

When on, individual side games (hole competitions, skins, etc.) can each have their own prize pool. When off, all prize money is consolidated to the main format purses.

### Official Event Mode

Under the **Other** section, **Official event mode** is the same setting flipped the other way. Turn it on for serious competitions where individual side-game purses should be disabled across the tournament. With Official event mode on, players cannot attach personal side-bet purses to games on this scorecard. Admins running a club championship, regional qualifier, or any event where house rules forbid side bets typically leave this on for the whole tournament.

---

# Scoring Formats

## Scoring Formats in Squabbit

Squabbit supports a wide range of golf scoring formats, from classic stroke play to complex team formats like 6-6-6. This section explains every format in detail so you know exactly how scores are calculated, what the organizer can configure, and what to expect on the scorecard.

## Understanding Games and the Scorecard

### What Is a "Game"?

A **game** is a scoring method attached to a round. Every time you enter scores on a scorecard, one or more games are running in the background, each computing your hole-by-hole and total scores according to their own rules.

A single scorecard can run **multiple games at the same time**. For example, a round might include a stroke play game, a skins game, and a Stableford game all at once. Each game tracks its own scores independently, and you can tap between game tabs on the scorecard to see your standing in each.

Many game types are layered: a "main" scoring format (like Best Ball or Nassau) sits on top of a "base" scoring format (like Stroke or Stableford). The base scoring calculates the raw hole score for each player, and the main format then aggregates or compares those raw scores to produce a team or match result. When you see a game like "Best Ball Stableford," the Stableford layer is the base that assigns points per hole, and Best Ball is the layer that picks the best score from the team.

### Limiting a Format to Specific Rounds

In a multi-round tournament, **any format can be scoped to just the rounds you choose**, not only Skins. When you edit a format, each of the tournament's rounds has an enable toggle; turn off the rounds you do not want it to include, and the format's leaderboard then aggregates only the enabled rounds.

This is how you build something like a "Low Net over Rounds 1 and 2 only" that sits alongside a separate overall format, or a different Skins pot per day. Add the format, enable just the rounds it should cover, and repeat with more scoped formats to cover whatever round combinations you want in the same tournament.

### Gross vs. Net

**Gross** means your actual stroke count on a hole. **Net** means your gross score minus any handicap strokes you receive on that hole. A player with a handicap of 18 on an 18-hole course receives one stroke on every hole, so their net score on each hole is one less than their gross score.

Most formats offer a gross mode and a net mode. When handicaps are enabled, calculations use net scores. When disabled, gross scores are used. In some team formats (like Best Ball), a **percentage** of each player's handicap is applied rather than the full handicap. The default is 85% in head-to-head team games and 75% in non-head-to-head team play.

Squabbit automatically shows a **gross scorecard tab** alongside any net game running in a tournament so you can always see raw stroke totals even when the main game is net-based.

### Gross on Par 3s

Some games offer a **"Gross on par 3s"** option. When enabled, handicap strokes are not applied on par-3 holes. Everyone plays those holes gross regardless of their handicap index. This is a common house rule in skins and similar formats.

## Stroke Play (Gross and Net)

Stroke play is the simplest format: count every shot, add them up, and the lowest total wins. Your score is your raw stroke count per hole, and totals are compared directly at the end.

In **gross** stroke play, scores are counted as played with no handicap adjustment. In **net** stroke play, handicap strokes are subtracted per hole before totaling. Net totals allow players of different abilities to compete fairly.

The leaderboard shows totals as stroke counts (e.g. "72") or relative to par (e.g. "-2", "+4", "E" for even par). The score-to-par view shows how far above or below par each player is.

**Key organizer settings:**
- Gross vs. net
- Handicap percentage per player
- Maximum hole score (see "Max Hole Score" below)
- Tiebreaker method

**Common questions:**
- "What does 'E' mean?" Even par: the player's total exactly equals par for the holes played.
- "Is the winning score plus or minus?" In stroke play, the lowest number wins. "-4" is better than "+2".

## Stableford

Stableford awards **points per hole** based on how your net score compares to par. Higher points are better, and the player or team with the most points wins.

**Default point values:**
- Albatross (3 under par or better): **5 points**
- Eagle (2 under par): **4 points**
- Birdie (1 under par): **3 points**
- Par: **2 points**
- Bogey (1 over par): **1 point**
- Double bogey or worse: **0 points**

For example: on a par-4 hole where you receive one handicap stroke, a gross 5 (one over gross par) becomes a net 4 (par), earning 2 points. A gross 4 (par gross) with one handicap stroke becomes a net 3 (birdie), earning 3 points.

Squabbit also supports a **score-to-par display mode** for Stableford. In this mode, the total shown is how far you are above or below the "expected" total of 2 points per hole played. After 9 holes earning 18 points, the display shows "E" (even). This makes it easier to compare players who have played different numbers of holes.

**Key organizer settings:**
- Points for each score type (albatross, eagle, birdie, par, bogey, double bogey, triple bogey, quadruple bogey, quintuple bogey) all individually editable
- Gross vs. net
- Max hole score
- **Rank by average points** (default on): When on, the tournament leaderboard ranks players by **average points per hole played** instead of total points. This keeps players who started earlier from sitting at the top just because they have more holes in the bank. Turn it off if you want the leaderboard to rank by raw total points throughout the round; once everyone finishes, the order is the same either way.

**Common questions:**
- "Does a double bogey score zero or negative?" Zero by default. The organizer can configure negative values for double bogey and worse if desired.
- "Can birdie be worth 4 points?" Yes. The organizer can set any point value for any score type.

## Quota

Quota is a points-based format where each player has a **target** (their quota) based on their handicap, and the goal is to exceed it. On each hole, gross Stableford-style points are earned. At the end of the round, your final score is your total points **minus** your quota. A positive score means you beat your quota; negative means you fell short.

**Quota formula:**
- Standard: `(number of holes × 2) − playing handicap`
- Example: A 10-handicapper playing 18 holes has a quota of `(18 × 2) − 10 = 26`
- Example: A 10-handicapper playing 9 holes has a quota of `(9 × 2) − 10 = 8`

**Chicago format:** Adds 3 to the quota, making it slightly harder. The same 10-handicapper on 18 holes would have a quota of 29.

**Hole scoring uses gross scores with these default point values (all configurable):**
- Albatross: 16 points
- Eagle: 8 points
- Birdie: 4 points
- Par: 2 points
- Bogey: 1 point
- Double bogey or worse: 0 points

Note that Quota scores holes on your **gross** score. Your handicap affects only the quota target, not per-hole scoring. This differs from Stableford, which uses net scores per hole.

**Key organizer settings:**
- Chicago format toggle (adds 3 to all quotas)
- Configurable point values per score type
- Manual quota override for individual players
- Tiebreaker method

**Common questions:**
- "My quota feels too easy. Can it be changed?" Yes. The organizer can set a custom quota for any player.
- "Why is my score negative?" You earned fewer points than your quota target.
- "Does handicap matter per hole?" No. It only determines your quota target.

## Match Play (Individual and Team)

Match play is a head-to-head format scored **hole by hole**. On each hole, the player or team with the better score wins the hole. If scores are equal, the hole is **halved** (tied) and nothing changes. Your standing is tracked as a running total: "2 up" means you have won 2 more holes than your opponent; "AS" (all square) means the counts are equal.

A match can be won before all holes are played. If you lead by more holes than remain, the match is over. For example, being 3 up with 2 holes left means you win "3&2" (3 up with 2 to play).

In **individual match play**, one player faces another. In **team match play**, two teams compete using their composite score (from Best Ball, Scramble, or another team format) to determine each hole's winner.

The scorecard shows the running cumulative match score next to each hole (e.g. "1up", "AS", "2dn"). The overall result displays as "W3&2" (won 3&2), "L2&1" (lost 2&1), or "AS" (all square).

**Key organizer settings:**
- Net vs. gross
- Handicap usage: full handicap or compare-with-lowest (where the lower-handicap player gives strokes equal to the handicap difference)
- Win/loss/tie points for tournament leaderboard (default: win=1, tie=0.5, loss=0)
- Optional per-hole points (points for winning individual holes)
- Optional separate front 9 and back 9 point values
- Match-level tiebreaker for elimination bracket use
- **Holes that count**: Pick a subset of holes on the round. Holes outside the selection do not affect the match result. Available on Match Play, Singles Match Play, and Low Ball / High Ball as well, matching the same option already offered for stroke-style formats.

**Match tiebreak:** Match Play and Elimination formats have a separate **Match tiebreak** setting alongside the overall tournament tiebreaker. This one decides what happens when an individual match ends all square after the final hole. Open the format edit page and tap **Match tiebreak** to choose one of the standard methods (sudden death from hole 1, back-from-18, lowest stroke index, low handicap, etc.). Each option has its own description in the picker. Configure this separately from the tournament-level tiebreaker — they serve different purposes.

**Carry over ties between rounds:** For multi-round Skins and Team Skins formats, a **Carry over ties between rounds** toggle controls whether tied holes that did not produce a winner in an earlier round roll forward to inflate the next round's skin values, or stay unclaimed. Without it, every new round starts fresh.

**Common questions:**
- "What does 'halved' mean?" Both players tied that hole. Neither gains or loses ground in the match.
- "Why did the match end early?" One player's lead was larger than the remaining holes, so the match was clinched before finishing.
- "In compare-with-lowest mode, who gives strokes?" The higher-handicap player receives strokes equal to the difference in playing handicaps. For example, a 14-handicapper vs. a 6-handicapper: the 14-handicapper gets 8 strokes on the 8 hardest holes.

## Presses

A **press** is a side bet within a match-play game that starts mid-round. When a player is losing by a configured threshold (e.g. "2 down"), they can "press" — opening a new mini-match from that hole to the end of the round, independent of the original match. If the presser falls behind in the press as well, they can press again.

Presses are available only in **personal side games** (not in tournament format games).

**Press value modes:** Same as the original bet, half, double, escalating (doubles with each successive press), or a custom fixed value.

**Auto-press:** When enabled, presses trigger automatically when a player reaches the configured deficit. Settings include trigger threshold, maximum number of presses, whether re-pressing is allowed, and the latest hole after which no new presses may start.

## Skins (Individual and Team)

Skins is a per-hole competition where each hole is worth one "skin." The player or team with the **lowest net score** on a hole wins that skin outright. If two or more players tie for the lowest score, the skin is not won and it **carries over** to the next hole, making the next hole worth two skins, then three, and so on until someone wins outright.

**Individual Skins:** Each player competes against all others. The sole player with the lowest net score on a hole wins all skins at stake.

**Team Skins:** Each team's composite score (from the base format: Best Ball, Scramble, etc.) is compared. The team with the sole lowest score wins the skins.

**Key configurable settings:**
- **Carry-over** (default: on): Tied holes carry over. If disabled, tied holes have no winner and are simply not worth anything.
- **Require par or better:** A player must score net par or better to win. Bogeys don't win skins.
- **Require birdie or better:** Must make a net birdie or better.
- **Natural birdies:** When multiple players tie with a net birdie (or better), the lowest gross score wins. A player who made a gross birdie beats a player whose net birdie came from a handicap stroke; tied gross scores still split the skin.
- **Multiple hole winners:** If enabled, tied players can share a skin instead of carrying over.
- **Gross beats net:** If a player's gross score equals their net score on a hole (they did not use a handicap stroke), that gross score is preferred over an equal net score that did require a handicap stroke.
- **Gross on par 3s:** Par-3 holes are scored gross for all players regardless of handicap.
- **Holes that count:** Skins are only contested on the holes you've selected to count. Holes outside the selection are never worth a skin, and a carry-over picks up on the next hole that does count.
- **Validate skins:** A birdie skin is held pending until the winner plays their next hole. If they make par or better on that hole, the skin is confirmed (green checkmark). If they bogey, the skin is forfeited and carries over (red). A skin won on the final hole remains pending (orange).

**Common questions:**
- "Why did hole 7 not have a winner even though I had the lowest score?" Another player may have matched your score, triggering a carry-over.
- "What happens to unclaimed skins at the end of the round?" They remain unclaimed. In multi-round tournaments, they carry over to the next round.

## Nassau

Nassau divides an 18-hole round into **three separate mini-matches**: the **front 9** (holes 1-9), the **back 9** (holes 10-18), and the **full 18**. Each mini-match is scored independently. You earn points based on who wins each segment, and it is possible to win some segments and lose others.

Nassau requires a base scoring format to evaluate each segment. The two sides' totals for each 9-hole window and the full 18 are compared separately.

**Default points:** Win = 2 points, tie = 0 points, loss = 0 points, for each of the three segments independently (all values are configurable).

An optional **per-hole points** setting adds points for winning individual holes as well.

**Key organizer settings:**
- Win/tie/loss points for front 9, back 9, and full 18, each configurable independently
- Optional per-hole win/tie/loss points
- Base scoring format (Stroke, Best Ball Stroke, Stableford, etc.)
- Net vs. gross

When the same two teams meet in more than one match in a round (for example a team-vs-team Nassau with multiple pairings), each match's Nassau points are **summed** into the team's total for the round rather than only the last match counting.

**Common questions:**
- "Nassau is sometimes called '2-2-2' — what does that mean?" 2 points for the front win, 2 for the back, 2 for the total. Those are the defaults.
- "Can a team win two segments but lose overall?" Yes. Each segment is evaluated independently.

## Best Ball

In Best Ball, each player plays their own ball for the full round and the **best individual score on each hole** from the team counts as the team's score. All other players' scores on that hole are discarded.

Best Ball requires a base scoring format. With Stroke as the base, the lowest stroke count per hole is best. With Stableford, the highest point total per hole is best.

**Default handicap:** 85% of each player's handicap in head-to-head play; 75% in non-head-to-head play.

**Key organizer settings:**
- Number of scores to use per hole (default: 1 best score). Can be 2 or more (e.g. best 2 from a team of 4).
- Custom score count per individual hole.
- All birdies count: when more team members make birdies than the configured score count, all birdies count on that hole.
- Modified variation: when both teams tie on their best hole score, the second-best score from each team is automatically used as a tiebreaker.
- Multiply Stableford scores: when using Stableford as the base, scores are multiplied together rather than added.

**Common questions:**
- "Only one score counts per hole — does my birdie count if someone else also made birdie?" If the scores are tied, both are the "best" — only one counts as the team score, but both players are considered to have contributed.
- "Which player's score gets highlighted?" The player(s) whose score was used on each hole are highlighted on the scorecard.

## Best Balls

Best Balls is mechanically identical to Best Ball. Its name emphasizes that the organizer has specifically configured multiple scores per hole throughout the round (rather than just 1 best score).

### Number of Top Rounds to Use (multi-round)

For any multi-round tournament format, the format edit page has a **Number of top rounds to use** tile. By default, every round counts toward the total. Set it to a number to keep only that many best rounds per player or team across the tournament — for example, in a 4-round event, keeping the **3 best** rounds drops each player's worst score automatically.

### Top Player Scores Per Round (team-sum formats)

For team-sum formats (Team Sum, Team Quota, and similar), the format edit page also exposes **Top player scores per round**. This controls how many individual scores from the team count on each round. A team of 4 with **Top 3 player scores per round** keeps the three best individual rounds per round and drops the worst. By default all player scores count.

The two settings are different: **Number of top rounds to use** drops a player's worst rounds across the whole tournament; **Top player scores per round** drops the team's worst score within each round.

## Best Ball 1-2-3

In 1-2-3 Best Ball, the number of team scores that count automatically escalates every 6 holes:
- **Holes 1-6:** Only the **1 best** score from the team counts per hole.
- **Holes 7-12:** The **2 best** scores from the team are summed per hole.
- **Holes 13-18:** All **3 best** scores from the team are summed per hole.

This format rewards consistency throughout the round and makes the final six holes the most valuable segment, since all three scores contribute.

## Alternate Shot

Alternate Shot (Foursomes) is a two-player team format where both partners **share one ball**. Partners take turns hitting shots throughout the hole. One player tees off on odd-numbered holes; the other tees off on even-numbered holes. They continue alternating shots within each hole until the ball is holed.

The team records one shared score per hole. Both players share a combined scorecard view.

**Default handicap:** 60% of one player's index + 40% of the other's, applied as a combined team handicap.

**Common questions:**
- "Does it matter who tees off first?" Yes. You agree before the round who tees off on hole 1, and the odd/even split follows automatically.

## Scramble

In a Scramble, all players tee off, the team selects the **best drive**, and all players play their next shot from that spot. The team selects the best shot again, and this continues until the ball is holed. One combined team score is recorded per hole.

The scorecard shows a combined round view (all team members share the same round). Scramble needs a base scoring format (Stroke or Stableford) for the leaderboard.

**Default handicap contribution percentages (applied then summed into a team handicap):**
- 4-person team: 25%, 20%, 15%, 10% from lowest to highest handicap.
- 2-person team: 35%, 15%.

## Shamble

A Shamble combines the Scramble tee shot with individual play. All players tee off, the team selects the **best drive**, and then each player plays their **own ball** from that spot for the rest of the hole. Each player records an individual score from the drive location.

The Shamble then scores like Best Ball: the best individual score (or best N, if configured) from that drive spot counts as the team's hole score. Individual scorecard rounds are shown separately (not combined like a Scramble).

## Ambrose

Ambrose is a Scramble with **handicaps enabled by default**. The mechanics are identical to Scramble. The only difference is that Ambrose applies each player's handicap to produce a team net handicap, making it fairer for groups of mixed ability.

**Common questions:**
- "What is the difference between Scramble and Ambrose?" Both play from the best drive. Ambrose includes handicap adjustments by default; a basic Scramble does not.
- "What is the difference between Shamble and Scramble?" In Scramble, every shot during the hole is selected from the best position. In Shamble, only the tee shot is shared; each player then plays their own ball for the rest of the hole.

## Chapman and Greensomes

**Chapman** (also called Pinehurst) is a two-player format. Both players tee off, then play each other's ball for the second shot. After the second shot, they select the best of the two balls and play alternate shot from there until the ball is holed.

**Greensomes** is similar but the team selects the best drive first (like a Shamble), then alternates shots from that point to finish the hole.

Both Chapman and Greensomes use a combined team scorecard like Alternate Shot. Default handicaps follow USGA recommendations for two-player team play.

## Team Average

Team Average computes the **average individual score** across the team. Each player plays and records their own round using the base scoring format. The team's score is the average of all players' totals, rounded to one decimal place.

**Configurable:** The organizer can specify that only the best N rounds count. For example, in a team of 4, only the top 3 scores might contribute, effectively dropping the worst individual round.

## SixSixSix (6-6-6)

6-6-6 is a team format where the **scoring format changes every 6 holes**. A round of 18 is divided into three segments:
- **Segment 1:** Holes 1-6
- **Segment 2:** Holes 7-12
- **Segment 3:** Holes 13-18

The organizer chooses a different format for each segment independently. Supported segment formats: Alternate Shot, Best Ball, Best Balls, Chapman, Greensomes, Singles Matchplay, Scramble, Shamble, and Worst Ball.

**Total score modes:**
- **Points per match** (default): Each segment is an independent match. Win, loss, or tie points are awarded per segment result. The overall total shows as a number (e.g. "2", "1.5"). Default per-segment points: win=1, tie=0.5, loss=0 (configurable).
- **Cumulative:** Scores carry continuously across all 18 holes without resetting. This mode shows a running matchplay total ("3up", "AS") and works best when all segments use the same compatible format.

Handicap settings, point values, and the base scoring format can be configured independently for each of the three segments.

On the scorecard, chips for Segment 1, 2, 3, and Final are shown. Tapping a segment displays that segment's scores. The scorecard auto-advances to the next segment after the final hole of the current one is entered.

**Common questions:**
- "Why does my 6-6-6 score show '2' instead of '2up'?" The game is in Points Per Match mode, where each segment awards flat win/tie/loss points rather than a running matchplay total.
- "Can each segment use different handicap percentages?" Yes. Handicap settings are configured per segment independently.
- "Can one segment be individual matchplay while another is Best Ball?" Yes. Singles Matchplay is one of the supported segment types.

## 3-3-3

3-3-3 is identical to 6-6-6 but uses **3-hole segments** designed for 9-hole rounds. Segments cover holes 1-3, 4-6, and 7-9. On an 18-hole course, the organizer selects either the front or back 9. All other rules, format options, handicap settings, and total score modes work exactly as in 6-6-6.

## Sixes

Sixes is a **4-player individual game** where players rotate partners every 6 holes. Over 18 holes, each player partners with each of the other three players for exactly one 6-hole segment. Within each segment, the two pairs play a 6-hole match against each other.

Sixes is an individual (not team) game: each player earns their own points, and the player with the most accumulated points across all three segments wins.

**Scoring:**
- Each 6-hole segment is played as a mini-match between two pairs.
- After each segment, the winning pair's players each earn match-result points. Default: win=1, tie=0.5, loss=0 (configurable).
- A player's round total is the sum of points from all three segment matches.

**Per-hole points option:** The organizer can also award points for winning individual holes within a segment. Hole wins are decided head-to-head: the two pairs' scores on the hole are compared against each other, not against par.

The scorecard shows each player's current partner's initials alongside their hole score.

**Key organizer settings:**
- Win/tie/loss points per segment
- Optional per-hole points
- Base scoring format (typically Stroke or Stableford)
- Handicap settings (defaults to compare-with-lowest)

**Common questions:**
- "My partner changes after hole 6 — is that predetermined?" Yes. Partnerships are set during match setup before the round.
- "I see matchplay scores during the segment — is that right?" Yes. Within each 6-hole segment, the two pairs play head-to-head match play.

## Nines

Nines is a **3-player points game** where 9 points are at stake on every hole. Players are ranked by their net score on each hole and points are distributed as follows:

**Standard allocation:**
- Sole low score: **5 points**
- Middle score: **3 points**
- Sole high score: **1 point**

**When players tie:**
- All three tie: **3 points** each
- Two tie for first: **4 points** each to the tied leaders, **1 point** to the sole last-place player
- Two tie for last: **5 points** to the sole leader, **2 points** each to the tied last-place players

This game requires **exactly 3 players** to function.

**Blitz rule (optional):** If the lowest score is 2 or more strokes better than the second-lowest score, the leader wins all 9 points and the other two players get 0 each.

**Key organizer settings:**
- Blitz toggle
- Handicap (defaults to net)

**Common questions:**
- "What if two players tie for the lowest score?" They each earn 4 points and the third player earns 1.
- "What if all three tie?" Each player earns 3 points.

## Best Front Nine / Best Back Nine

These formats score only **one half** of an 18-hole round:
- **Best Front Nine:** Only holes 1-9 contribute to the score.
- **Best Back Nine:** Only holes 10-18 contribute.

Players play all 18 holes, but only the specified 9 count toward the leaderboard. The base scoring format (Stroke, Stableford, etc.) determines how hole scores within the window are calculated.

Tiebreakers for these formats are restricted to the relevant 9-hole window.

## Elimination Bracket (Individual and Team)

The Elimination format is a **knockout bracket tournament**. Players (or teams) face each other in head-to-head matches. Winners advance; losers are eliminated. The last player or team standing wins.

**Bracket structure:**
- The organizer seeds players into the bracket manually or through automatic scheduling.
- If the number of players is not a power of 2, some players receive a **bye** (automatic advancement without playing) in the first round.
- Each match is scored as standard match play (individual or team).
- After all matches in a round are scored, winners automatically advance to the next round.

**Editing the bracket (admin):** The bracket is editable directly from the **Schedule tab**. Tap the **Bracket** chip to open it. From there you tap any slot to fill or change it:
- An empty slot shows **Add player** (or **Add team**) and opens a picker to seed that spot. The **+** button auto-seeds the round for you.
- A filled slot lets you swap in a different player/team or remove the current one. For individual brackets you can also view the player's profile.
- If you edit a match that already has scores entered, Squabbit asks you to confirm first. When you change an early-round slot, that player is also cleared from any later rounds so the bracket stays consistent.

Players and spectators see the bracket as **read-only**: match cards with names and avatars, round labels (Preliminary, Quarter Final, Semi Final, Final), BYE slots, scores with a checkmark next to the winner, and a play button to open the scorecard when a tee time exists.

**Bracket layout:** The format has a **Bracket layout** setting with two options: **Left to right** (every round flows from left to right) and **Out to in** (the two halves of the draw start on the outer left and right edges and converge on the final in the center, like a TV-style draw). The chosen layout applies everywhere the bracket is shown: in the app, on the public website, and on the printed bracket PDF.

**Printable bracket:** Admins can print the bracket as a PDF from **Edit Tournament → Print tournament assets** by choosing the **Bracket** asset. See the PDFs and Printing guide for what it includes.

**When a match ends all square** (tied after all holes), an optional **per-match tiebreaker** resolves the result:
- **Countback:** Compare scores on the last 9, 6, 3, and 1 holes using course holes 10-18 (or 1-9 for 9-hole rounds starting on hole 1).
- **Dynamic countback:** Same but uses each player's own last 9, 6, 3, 1 holes played (for shotgun starts).
- **Back from 18:** Compare hole-by-hole from hole 18 backward until a winner is found.
- **Forward from 1:** Compare hole-by-hole from hole 1 forward.
- **From lowest stroke index to highest:** Compare starting with the hardest hole (SI 1) and working to the easiest.
- **Low handicap:** The player with the lower playing handicap wins.

**Leaderboard:** Players accumulate points per round (default: win=1, tie=0.5, loss=0). The "use match result as points" option lets the actual match score (e.g. holes up) count directly as points instead of a flat win value.

## Round Robin (Individual and Team)

A Round Robin schedules every player (or team) to face every other player in their flight across multiple rounds. No one is eliminated, and final standings are determined by total accumulated points.

**Match scheduling:** Squabbit uses the **circle method algorithm** to generate pairings, guaranteeing no repeat matchups for up to N-1 rounds with N players. In two-team formats (like Ryder Cup individual sessions), a bipartite method ensures players only face opponents from the opposing team.

**Points:** Default: win=1, tie=0.5, loss=0 (all configurable). Points accumulate across all rounds.

If the number of players is odd in a round, one player receives a bye for that round.

## Ryder Cup

The Ryder Cup format in Squabbit is a **multi-session team competition** between two large groups (Team A and Team B). Over multiple tournament rounds (sessions), various game formats are played, typically a mix of team matchplay formats (Best Ball, Alternate Shot, Scramble) and individual singles matchplay rounds.

**How scoring works:**
- Each individual match within a session contributes points toward its team's running total.
- Win/tie/loss points per match are configurable (default: win=1, tie=0.5, loss=0).
- The team with the most accumulated points across all sessions wins.
- Individual player contribution points and overall team totals are both shown on the leaderboard.

**What the organizer configures:**
- Game format per session (Best Ball, Alternate Shot, Scramble, Singles Matchplay, etc.)
- Individual match pairings per round (who faces whom)
- Win/tie/loss points per match
- Optional per-hole win/tie/loss points for additional granularity

## Net vs. Gross: Auto-Generated Gross View

In any tournament running a net-based format, Squabbit automatically displays a **"Gross" scorecard tab** alongside the main net game. This tab shows raw stroke counts without handicap adjustments. It is a display overlay, not a separate competition, and does not affect leaderboard standings.

For non-tournament (side) games, you choose whether to enable handicaps when creating the game. The setting can be changed later in the game edit screen.

## Max Hole Score Settings

The organizer can set a **maximum score per hole** to cap how high any player's score on a single hole can be. This prevents one disastrous hole from unfairly ruining an otherwise good round.

**Where to find it:** Max hole score is configured **per format**. From the tournament edit page, open the **Setup tab > Formats > tap the format name > Max hole score**. Each format has its own cap, so a stroke play format can cap at net double bogey while a skins format on the same tournament counts every stroke. (Before app version 6.6.0 this was one tournament-wide setting; it now lives on each format, so caps set in older versions need to be re-set on the format.)

**Available options:**
- **Gross double bogey:** Cap at par + 2 (ignoring handicap strokes).
- **Gross triple bogey:** Cap at par + 3.
- **Gross quadruple bogey:** Cap at par + 4.
- **Net double bogey:** Cap at par + 2 + handicap strokes received on that hole. This is the standard USGA/WHS approach (also called ESC).
- **Net triple bogey:** Cap at par + 3 + handicap strokes on that hole.
- **Net quadruple bogey:** Cap at par + 4 + handicap strokes.
- **Double par (gross):** Cap at 2 × the hole's par.
- **Net double par:** Cap at 2 × (hole par + handicap strokes received on that hole).

**Example:** On a par-5 where a player receives 1 handicap stroke, "Net double bogey" caps the score at 5 + 2 + 1 = 8 strokes. If the player took 12, their score is entered as 8.

A **net** cap works even on a format that is scored gross. Squabbit still looks up each player's handicap strokes on the hole to compute the cap (par + N + strokes), so you can apply a net double-bogey-style cap to a gross stroke-play format without switching the format to net.

The cap applies to the underlying stroke count before Stableford, Quota, or other point calculations run. So if a player is capped at net double bogey, the point calculation uses the capped strokes, not the actual strokes.

## Tiebreakers

When players or teams are tied in total score on the tournament leaderboard at the end, the organizer can configure a tiebreaker to determine final placements. The tiebreaker uses the **final round** of the tournament for comparison. Raw scores (not score-to-par-adjusted) are always used to prevent formats like Stableford from inverting the comparison.

A star (*) appears next to tied entries. Tapping the entry reveals exactly how the tiebreaker was applied, step by step.

**Available tiebreaker methods:**

- **Countback (traditional):** Compare scores on the last 9 holes (holes 10-18 by default, or 1-9 for 9-hole rounds starting on hole 1), then last 6 (holes 13-18), last 3 (holes 16-18), then just hole 18. The player with the better score in the first segment where they differ wins.

- **Dynamic countback:** Same concept, but each player's own finishing hole determines their window. Relevant for shotgun starts where players finish on different holes.

- **Back from 18:** Compare hole-by-hole from hole 18 backward until a difference is found.

- **Forward from 1:** Compare hole-by-hole from hole 1 forward until a difference is found.

- **From lowest stroke index to highest:** Start with the hardest hole (stroke index 1) and work to the easiest (stroke index 18). The player with the better score on the hardest hole wins.

- **Low handicap:** The player with the lower playing handicap is ranked higher. No score comparison is used.

**Tiebreaker pending:** If the final round has not been scored yet, the tiebreaker shows as "pending" and resolves once scores are entered.

**Manual winner override:** The organizer can always designate a manual winner for any tied placement, overriding the automatic tiebreaker.

## Linking a Tournament Format to a League Format

When the tournament is part of a league, the format edit page has a **League** section listing every league format the parent league runs. Each league format gets its own **Count towards {league format name}** toggle.

By default a new tournament format counts toward every applicable league format (all individual league formats for individual tournament formats, all team league formats for team tournament formats). Turn a toggle off when you want a specific tournament format excluded from a specific league format — for example, a "Fun Scramble" at the holiday tournament that you do not want polluting the league's Sum standings, but you still want the Stableford from the same tournament to count.

The toggle is independent per league format, so a tournament running both an individual and a team format can count toward one but not the other.

## Hole Events

Hole Events are **custom per-hole achievements** that award or deduct points for specific occurrences during a round. They run as an overlay on any main game and accumulate a separate running total across the round.

**Individual player events:**
- **Birdie or better (net):** Net birdie or better on the hole.
- **Gross birdie or better:** Gross birdie or better (no handicap applied).
- **Birdie (exact, net):** Exactly a net birdie.
- **Eagle or better (net):** Net eagle (-2) or better.
- **Hole in one:** Gross stroke count of 1.
- **Par or better (net):** Net par or better.
- **Par (exact, net):** Exactly net par.
- **Bogey (exact, net):** Exactly one over net par.
- **Double / triple / quadruple / quintuple bogey:** Exact over-par amounts (net).
- **One putt:** Exactly 1 putt (requires putt tracking enabled).
- **Three putt / four putt:** Exactly 3 or 4 putts (requires putt tracking).
- **Greenie:** Hit the green in regulation. On a par-3, this means the tee shot; on a par-4, the second shot; on a par-5, the third shot. Requires GIR tracking.
- **Sandie:** Made par or better after hitting a bunker shot (requires bunker shot tracking).
- **Chippie:** Chipped in for the score (requires chip-in tracking).
- **Barkie:** Made par or better after hitting a tree (manually awarded per hole by tapping the scorecard cell).
- **Closest to the pin in regulation:** Manually awarded per hole.
- **Longest drive:** Manually awarded per hole.
- **Lowest score:** The player with the lowest net score on the hole wins automatically.
- **Par scramble:** Made par without hitting the green in regulation (requires GIR tracking).
- **Custom:** A custom-named event with any point value, awarded manually per hole.

**Team events (comparing the two teams on a hole):**
- **High ball:** Each team's highest-scoring player is compared; the team whose "worst" player scored lower wins.
- **Low combined:** The team with the lower combined (summed) score on the hole wins.
- **Fewest combined putts:** The team with fewer total putts wins (requires putt tracking).

**Default point values:** Positive events default to +1 (birdie, greenie, etc.). Penalty events default to -1 (three putt, bogey, double bogey, etc.). All values are configurable.

**Additional Hole Events options:**
- **Multiple winners:** Whether more than one player can win the same event on the same hole.
- **Points cancel out:** Winning and losing points on the same hole from different events cancel each other.
- **Multiply by hole number:** Each event's points are multiplied by the current hole number (hole 1 = 1×, hole 18 = 18×), making later holes more valuable.
- **Umbriago:** If one player wins every event active on a hole, their points for that hole are doubled. A minimum points threshold for umbriago eligibility can be configured.

Hole Events totals are tracked separately from the main game score and can be used as the basis for side bet payouts.

---

# Handicaps

## What Is a Handicap?

A **handicap** is a number that represents roughly how many strokes above (or below) par a golfer typically shoots, adjusted for course difficulty. It is the great equalizer in golf: a beginner with a handicap of 24 and a low-single-digit player can compete fairly in the same round because the beginner receives extra strokes. Without handicaps, the beginner would almost never beat the expert; with them, the match is genuinely contested.

In Squabbit, your handicap appears on your profile and flows automatically into every tournament, league, and casual game you join. When handicaps are enabled, the app distributes your strokes across holes and adjusts your score. You never need to do the arithmetic yourself.

## Your Handicap Index: Three Ways to Set It

Your **Handicap Index** is the single number stored on your profile. It is your portable skill rating. Squabbit supports three ways to maintain it.

### Manual

You type a number and it stays fixed until you update it. Good for players who have a club handicap but do not have a GHIN or WHS number, or who just want a stable starting point. To set it: go to your profile, tap **Handicap Index**, tap **Source**, choose **Manual**, then tap the displayed handicap value to edit it.

### Squabbit (Auto-calculated from Your Rounds)

Squabbit tracks every round you record and recalculates your index automatically using the WHS formula after every completed round. No registration or setup with an external body is needed. This is the default for new accounts. Your index updates within seconds of submitting a scorecard.

When you have this source selected, tapping your handicap value on your profile opens a detailed breakdown of exactly which rounds are being used and why.

If you have not yet completed a round, you can set a temporary starting handicap by entering your lowest recent score when prompted, or by going to your profile and manually setting one. The temporary value is replaced once you have enough round data.

### GHIN / WHS (Official Handicap Link)

If you have an official handicap registered with a national golf association (in the US this is GHIN, elsewhere it is the country's WHS-compliant system) you can link your account. Squabbit fetches your current official index every time you open the app and every time you are added to a tournament or league event.

To link your account:

1. Go to your profile and tap **Handicap Index**.
2. Tap **Source** and choose **GHIN or WHS**.
3. Select your **country or region** from the list.
4. Enter your **membership or GHIN number**. For most countries this is numeric; for Chile and Spain it may contain letters.
5. Squabbit looks up your current index immediately and shows a confirmation.

**Countries with live automatic lookup supported:** United States (via GHIN), Canada, Chile, Spain, Africa (South Africa via handicaps.co.za), China, India, Mexico, and UAE.

**Countries listed but without live lookup:** Australia, Ireland, New Zealand, and the United Kingdom. These countries do not make handicap data publicly accessible via an API. For players in these regions, you can use Squabbit's built-in calculation or set your handicap manually.

**Important:** Squabbit can only **read** your handicap index from your linked account. It cannot submit scores to GHIN or any other official system.

To manually refresh your WHS/GHIN handicap at any time, go to your profile, tap **Handicap Index**, and tap the **Refresh** button next to your handicap value.

## Which Rounds Count Toward Your Squabbit Index

Every round you save in Squabbit defaults to **counting toward your handicap**. Squabbit will include that round in its World Handicap System calculation of your Index.

You can exclude any individual round from the calculation so it has no effect on your Squabbit Index. The round still saves with all your scores and stats; it just isn't fed into the WHS formula.

### How to exclude a round from your handicap

1. Open the scorecard for that round.
2. Tap your **name or avatar** on your row to open the per-player options sheet.
3. Turn off **Count towards handicap**.

You can flip the toggle back on any time and the round will re-enter your calculation immediately.

### When to turn it off

- Practice rounds
- Scrambles, best ball, and other team formats where your individual score isn't a real measure of your play
- Rounds you played from non-standard tees or on incomplete holes
- Any round you don't want affecting your Squabbit Index for any reason

This setting only affects your **Squabbit-calculated Index**. If your handicap source is GHIN or WHS, Squabbit pulls your Index from the external system and the toggle has no effect on what's shown on your profile.

## Handicap Index vs. Course Handicap

These two terms cause the most confusion, and misunderstanding them is the most common reason players wonder why their net score looks different from what they expected.

- Your **Handicap Index** is the portable number on your profile. Think of it as your overall skill rating independent of any specific course.
- Your **Course Handicap** is what you actually play to on a given course and tee on a given day. It accounts for the difficulty of the specific course and tee you are playing from.

### Why They Are Different

Golf courses vary enormously in difficulty. A Handicap Index of 10 earned on an easy resort course does not automatically translate to 10 strokes of allowance when you play a demanding championship layout. The course handicap conversion levels the field by giving you more strokes on a harder course and fewer on an easier one.

### The Standard USGA Formula

**Course Handicap = Index × (Slope Rating ÷ 113) + (Course Rating − Par)**

- **Slope Rating** measures the relative difficulty of a course for a bogey golfer compared to a scratch golfer. The neutral value is 113.
- **Course Rating** is the expected score for a scratch golfer from those tees.
- **Par** is the standard score for the course.

**Worked example:** Your Handicap Index is 12.0. You are playing the blue tees (Slope 128, Rating 71.8, Par 72).

- 12.0 × (128 ÷ 113) = 12.0 × 1.133 = 13.6
- 13.6 + (71.8 − 72) = 13.6 − 0.2 = 13.4
- Course Handicap = 13 (rounded to nearest whole number at the final step)

You receive 13 strokes, even though your index is 12, because those tees are harder than the neutral standard.

**Same player, different tees:** White tees (Slope 110, Rating 69.5, Par 72):

- 12.0 × (110 ÷ 113) = 12.0 × 0.973 = 11.7
- 11.7 + (69.5 − 72) = 11.7 − 2.5 = 9.2
- Course Handicap = 9

You receive only 9 strokes from those easier tees. This makes it fair when players in the same group choose different tees.

### Regional Formula Variants

Squabbit supports four course handicap formulas. Tournament and league organizers choose which one applies:

| Formula | Description |
|---|---|
| **USGA** (default) | Index × (Slope ÷ 113) + (Rating − Par) |
| **UK** | Same calculation as USGA |
| **Australian** | Same as USGA multiplied by 0.93 with a gender-based consistency factor |
| **South African** | Same base formula, but rounds the course handicap before applying any percentage allowance, then rounds again after the percentage |

If you are unsure, USGA is correct for the vast majority of players worldwide.

## Gross vs. Net Scoring

Every game in Squabbit has a **handicap usage** setting that determines how strokes are applied. There are three options.

### Gross

Handicaps are not used. Only raw scores count. Use this for open scratch events or when all players are at a similar level and no adjustment is needed.

### Net Score

The most common mode. Your handicap strokes are distributed across holes according to each hole's **Stroke Index** (see next section). For each hole where you receive a stroke, your raw score is reduced by one before it is entered into the scoring calculation.

**Example:** Two players tee off on a par-4 hole with Stroke Index 3.

- Player A has a course handicap of 4. Their handicap (4) is greater than the hole's SI (3), so they receive one stroke here. They score 5 gross. Net score = 5 − 1 = 4 (net par).
- Player B has a course handicap of 8. Their handicap (8) is also greater than SI 3, so they also receive one stroke. They score 6 gross. Net score = 6 − 1 = 5 (net bogey).

### Compare with Lowest (Net Off Lowest)

Instead of each player using their full handicap, Squabbit finds the **lowest playing handicap** in the game and assigns that player zero extra strokes. Everyone else receives only the **difference** between their handicap and the lowest.

**Example:** Player A has a course handicap of 5, Player B has 9, Player C has 13.

- Lowest = 5 (Player A's handicap)
- Player A receives 0 strokes (5 − 5)
- Player B receives 4 strokes (9 − 5)
- Player C receives 8 strokes (13 − 5)

The 4 or 8 extra strokes are assigned to the hardest holes by stroke index. Player A plays scratch; Player C gets help only on the 8 hardest holes.

This mode is typical for matchplay competitions. The best player plays from scratch and the other players only receive strokes relative to that standard, keeping matches competitive without over-inflating the advantage of high-handicap players.

## How Strokes Are Assigned to Holes (Stroke Index)

Every hole on a course has a **Stroke Index (SI)** number, usually ranked 1 through 18. SI 1 is the hardest hole for stroke-allocation purposes; SI 18 is the easiest.

When your course handicap is, say, 14, you receive **one stroke** on each of the 14 holes with the lowest (hardest) stroke index numbers, and nothing on the remaining 4 holes.

If your handicap exceeds 18, you start receiving a **second stroke** on the hardest holes. A handicap of 22 gives you one stroke on every hole plus a second stroke on the 4 holes with SI 1 through 4.

**Plus-handicap players** (those who typically shoot under par) give strokes back to the course. Their strokes come off the easiest holes first, the holes with the highest SI numbers. A +2 handicap means you give a stroke back on SI 17 and SI 18.

Squabbit handles all this automatically. Dots on the scorecard indicate how many strokes you receive on each hole (one dot for one stroke, two dots for two strokes, a circle for a stroke you give back on a plus handicap).

### Second Stroke Index (SI 19-36)

Some courses, common in Australia, publish a separate order for allocating the 19th through 36th strokes instead of looping back through the primary stroke index. Squabbit supports this: in the course editor, tap a hole's S.I. cell and turn on **Uses second stroke index** to record values 19-36 alongside the primary 1-18. When every hole being played has a second stroke index, playing handicaps above 18 allocate their extra strokes in that published order. When a course has no second stroke index, strokes past 18 fall back to the primary order (a second stroke on SI 1 first, and so on).

### Gross Scores on Par-3 Holes

Some formats, particularly certain Skins and Stableford variants, include a setting called **Gross scores on par-3s**. When enabled, handicap strokes are not applied on any par-3 hole regardless of that hole's stroke index. Your score on par-3s is always gross. Organizers set this toggle when creating the game format.

## 9-Hole Auto-Halving

When a game is set to 9 holes, Squabbit automatically halves your full course handicap to produce your 9-hole playing handicap.

If your full handicap is **odd**, the extra half-stroke goes to whichever 9-hole half contains the hole with Stroke Index 1 (the hardest hole). This means the harder 9 always gets the benefit of the odd stroke.

**Example:** Course handicap = 15. SI 1 is on the front nine.

- Front nine: playing handicap = 8 (half of 15, rounded up, because SI 1 is on the front)
- Back nine: playing handicap = 7 (the remaining half)

If SI 1 were on the back nine instead, the values would flip.

When the app cannot determine which 9 is being played (no starting hole information), it simply divides by 2 without the odd-stroke adjustment.

On 18-hole courses, each tee can optionally store a dedicated **Front 9** and **Back 9** rating and slope in the course editor. When those are set, a 9-hole round's course handicap conversion uses the nine's own rating and slope instead of deriving them from the full-18 values. Multi-nine courses (27+ holes) store a rating and slope per named nine per tee.

## Peoria Handicapping

**Peoria** is an on-the-day handicapping method designed for events where players either do not have established handicaps or where the organizer wants to level the field without relying on outside systems.

### How It Works

Before play begins, the organizer secretly selects **6 holes** from the 18 (or **3 holes** for a 9-hole round). Players do not know which holes were selected until after they finish their round.

Once the round is complete, each player's Peoria handicap is calculated:

**Peoria Handicap = (Score on secret holes × 3 − Course Par) × 80%**

This result becomes that player's playing handicap for the round. It is then applied exactly like any other handicap, with strokes distributed by hole SI across all 18 holes.

**Example:** The 6 secret holes have a total par of 22. A player scores a combined 31 on those 6 holes (9 over par on the secret selection):

- 31 × 3 = 93
- 93 − 72 (full course par) = 21
- 21 × 0.80 = 16.8
- Peoria handicap ≈ 17

The ×3 extrapolation estimates what the player would have scored over 18 holes at the same rate as the 6 secret holes. The ×80% applies a built-in reduction to account for the fact that players naturally play harder when they know which holes are being measured.

### Secrecy and Transparency

Non-admin players see only their final Peoria handicap number. Tournament admins and organizers can see the breakdown: which specific holes were chosen, the strokes on each secret hole, the running total, and the full formula result.

### When to Use Peoria

Peoria works well for:
- Company or charity outings where most players lack official handicaps
- Scramble or fun events where you want an on-the-day leveler
- Any situation where you want to prevent players from inflating their handicap before the event (sandbagging), since they cannot know which holes count

To enable Peoria, the organizer selects **Peoria** as the course handicap formula in the game format settings and then picks the 6 (or 3) secret holes using the hole-selection grid.

## Squabbit's Built-in WHS Handicap Calculation

When your handicap source is set to **Squabbit**, the app follows the World Handicap System (WHS) methodology.

### Step 1: Handicap Differential per Round

For each completed round, Squabbit calculates a **Handicap Differential**:

**(Adjusted Gross Score − Tee Rating) × (113 ÷ Tee Slope)**

The "adjusted gross score" first caps each individual hole at **net double bogey** (par + 2 + any strokes you receive on that hole). This prevents one catastrophic hole from unfairly inflating your differential.

**Example:** You are playing a par-5 hole, SI 1, and your handicap is 15. You receive one stroke. Your maximum counted score is 5 + 2 + 1 = 8. You scored 12 on the hole. It counts as 8.

After capping all holes, the differential formula is applied using the rating and slope of the tees you played.

### Step 2: Pool of 20 Differentials

Squabbit tracks your 20 most recent completed rounds. **Nine-hole rounds are paired** with the next available 9-hole round to form a combined 18-hole equivalent differential. A lone 9-hole round waits for a partner before it joins the pool.

### Step 3: WHS Lookup Table

The number of best differentials to average is determined by how many you have accumulated:

- 3 or fewer rounds: average of 1 best, minus 2 adjustment
- 4 rounds: average of 1 best, minus 1 adjustment
- 5 rounds: average of 1 best, no adjustment
- 6 rounds: average of 2 best, minus 1 adjustment
- 7–8 rounds: average of 2 best, no adjustment
- 9–11 rounds: average of 3 best
- 12–14 rounds: average of 4 best
- 15–16 rounds: average of 5 best
- 17–18 rounds: average of 6 best
- 19 rounds: average of 7 best
- 20 rounds: average of 8 best (full WHS)

The negative adjustments for small pools are a WHS design choice to protect the field from players whose true handicap has not yet been established.

### Step 4: Exceptional Rounds

If you post a round whose differential is **7.0 or more below** your current Handicap Index, Squabbit automatically lowers the recorded differentials of your previous rounds by 1.0 stroke each. If the difference was 10.0 or more, the reduction is 2.0 strokes per previous round. This exceptional round mechanism is WHS-compliant and prevents one standout round from being completely diluted by a bank of older higher differentials.

In the handicap explanation dialog on your profile:
- An asterisk (*) after a differential means that round's differential was lowered by an exceptional round.
- A double asterisk (**) marks the exceptional round itself that triggered the adjustment.

### Worked Example from Scratch

You have 10 completed rounds with these differentials (sorted best first): 7.2, 8.1, 9.0, 9.8, 10.4, 11.2, 12.5, 13.1, 14.0, 15.6.

With 10 differentials, the WHS table says use the **3 best**, no adjustment:
- Average of 7.2, 8.1, 9.0 = 24.3 ÷ 3 = **8.1**
- Handicap Index = **8.1**

## Handicap Percentage Allowance

Organizers can apply a **percentage allowance** to scale all playing handicaps up or down. This is common when:

- The format benefits from reduced allowances (e.g., 85% in a 72-hole stroke play championship)
- USGA-recommended allowances for certain formats require it (e.g., 90% for Four-Ball stroke play)

The percentage is applied after the course handicap conversion.

**Example:** Course handicap = 20. Allowance = 85%.
- 20 × 0.85 = 17.0
- Playing handicap = 17

For team formats, individual percentages can be set per player position within a team. For example, in a Greensomes format, you might use 60% of the lower handicap and 40% of the higher. Each position in the team gets its own percentage. These are applied to individual player handicaps before the team handicap is computed by summing them.

## Maximum and Minimum Handicap Caps

A **maximum handicap** cap limits how many strokes any player can receive in a particular event. If your playing handicap (after conversion and any percentage) exceeds the cap, it is reduced to the cap value for that event only. Your profile index is not affected.

A **minimum handicap** floor works the opposite way. If your playing handicap falls below the floor, it is raised to the floor for that event. This is sometimes used in formats where plus-handicap players would otherwise have an unfair edge.

Both caps are applied after the course handicap step and percentage step, but before rounding. They appear as steps in the scorecard handicap explanation dialog.

### Per-Format Min/Max Handicap

In addition to the league-level cap, every tournament format has its own **Maximum handicap** and **Minimum handicap** tiles on the format edit page. They apply only inside that format, on top of any league cap that already exists.

Example: A league has a max handicap of 36 (so no player can receive more than 36 strokes in any event). One of the tournament's formats is Stableford and the admin wants to be stricter there — they set the format's max handicap to 24. Players with a course handicap above 24 are capped at 24 for the Stableford format only; the league's other formats still use the 36 cap. Useful when one specific format would be unbalanced with high handicaps but the rest of the field is fine.

## Team Handicap Calculation

In team formats where rounds are combined (scramble, Chapman, greensomes, etc.), Squabbit computes a **team handicap** by summing the percentage-adjusted individual handicaps.

For a scramble with four players each contributing 25% of their handicap:
- Player A course handicap 16 × 25% = 4.0
- Player B course handicap 12 × 25% = 3.0
- Player C course handicap 8 × 25% = 2.0
- Player D course handicap 20 × 25% = 5.0
- Team handicap = 14.0 → rounds to 14

In matchplay team formats (e.g., four-ball vs. game), Squabbit follows USGA guidance:

- In **singles matchplay**, the percentage is applied first, then each player's handicap is rounded individually, then the lowest is found and all players compare to it.
- In **four-ball and other team matchplay**, the comparison happens on unrounded course handicaps first, the percentage is applied to the difference, and the result is rounded. This matches the USGA Rules of Handicapping appendix on four-ball allowances.

### Manual Team Handicap Override

An organizer can bypass the automatic calculation and set a team's handicap manually for any event. The manual value is shown clearly in the playing handicap breakdown dialog on the scorecard.

## Tournament Handicap vs. League Handicap vs. Your Profile Index

Your profile **Handicap Index** updates continuously as you play. However, competitions typically lock in a handicap at a specific point to keep things fair throughout the event.

### Tournament Handicap

When you are added to a tournament, your current profile index is captured as your **Tournament Handicap** for that event. It does not change during the tournament even if your profile index changes mid-event. An admin can manually adjust a player's tournament handicap if needed.

For multi-round tournaments, the admin can also set a **per-round handicap override** that applies only to a specific round within the tournament.

### League Handicap

Leagues have three options for how handicaps are tracked:

- **Current Index:** Each time you are added to a new league event, your tournament handicap for that event is set to your current profile index. Your handicap evolves event by event.
- **Fixed Handicap:** Your handicap is locked to what it was when you joined the league. It stays the same for every event unless the admin changes it.
- **League Events:** Your handicap is calculated using only rounds you have played within the league (or selected league seasons). Rounds outside the league are not counted. This is useful for leagues that want a self-contained handicap system.

### Use WHS/GHIN Handicap When Available

For the **Fixed Handicap** and **League Events** modes, an extra toggle called **Use WHS/GHIN handicap when available** appears in the league settings. When it's on, any player who has linked a WHS or GHIN account uses their official handicap index instead of the handicap the league would otherwise compute (their fixed value or their league-rounds calculation). Players without a linked WHS/GHIN account keep using the league's handicap as normal. This toggle does not apply to **Current Index** mode, which already follows each player's profile index.

### League Handicap Formula (League Events mode only)

When the league handicap type is **League events**, an additional **Handicap formula** tile appears in the league settings. It controls exactly how the league turns league-round history into a current index. Two options:

- **WHS formula**: Uses the official World Handicap System lookup table to decide how many of a player's best rounds to average, based on how many total league rounds they have. This is the same formula Squabbit uses for personal handicaps.
- **Best of recent**: Pick **X best of the latest Y rounds**. Both X and Y are admin-editable (X must be less than or equal to Y). For example, **Best 5 of latest 10** averages a player's five best handicap differentials from their ten most recent league rounds. Useful for leagues that want a simpler, more responsive formula than full WHS.

### 9-Hole Rounds in League Handicaps (League Events mode only)

Also in **League events** mode, a **9 hole rounds** tile controls how 9-hole rounds feed into the league handicap. Two options:

- **Combine in pairs**: Two 9-hole rounds are combined to form one 18-hole score. A 9-hole round doesn't affect the handicap until a second 9-hole round is played. (If a player has only one 9-hole score so far, the league waits for a second one before updating their index.)
- **Count each round**: Every 9-hole round counts as its own score right away. Squabbit adds an **expected score** for the unplayed nine to turn it into a full 18-hole differential. The expected score follows the World Handicap System's 2024 treatment of 9-hole scores and is based on the player's handicap index at the time the round was played (the formula is expected score = (0.52 × handicap index) + 1.2). This option is recommended for leagues that play only 9-hole events, so a player's handicap updates after every round instead of every other round.

These 18-hole-equivalent rounds are marked with a † on the league handicap explanation dialog.

### Seasons to Include

Also in **League events** mode, a **Seasons to include** tile lets admins pull in handicap data from previous seasons of the same league. The subtitle reads **This season only** by default or **N seasons** once additional seasons are selected. Tap the tile to open a dialog with checkboxes for every past season — useful for the first event of a new season when a member has not yet played enough rounds in the current season to compute a stable index.

### Importing a Player's Historical Rounds into a League

When a brand-new member joins a league and you want their historical scores to feed the handicap pool, the league admin can bulk-upload that member's past round history from a spreadsheet. From the league settings, open the **Import player rounds** tile, pick the member from the player list, and upload their CSV. The imported rounds are attributed to that one player and tied to the league, so they count immediately in **League events** handicap calculations. This is different from the league-wide tournament import (which loads full event results); this one loads a single player's personal history.

### Viewing How a League Handicap Is Calculated

To see the step-by-step breakdown of a player's **league handicap**, open the event, tap the **gear icon**, go to the **Players** tab, and tap the player's handicap value on the Players page. This opens the league handicap explanation dialog, which shows exactly which league rounds were used, their differentials, the formula that was applied (WHS or Best of recent), and the resulting index. (18-hole-equivalent rounds built from combined 9-hole scores are marked with a †.)

This is the right place to answer "how is the league handicap calculated?" It is distinct from the two per-event views below: tapping a handicap on the scorecard (or the scorecard's info icon, or the playing handicap in the tournament round leaderboard) opens the **playing handicap** breakdown for that single event, and tapping your handicap on your profile opens your **profile index** breakdown. To change *how* the league computes handicaps, use the handicap type and formula tiles in the league settings (see the League Handicap sections above).

## Playing Handicap Explanation Dialog

Any time you are curious why your net score looks the way it does, tap your handicap number on the scorecard. A step-by-step dialog appears showing every factor:

- Starting handicap index (or tournament/round override if applicable)
- Course handicap conversion (with slope, rating, and par values shown)
- 9-hole halving (if applicable)
- Percentage allowance (if applicable)
- Max/min cap (if applicable)
- Rounding step
- Compare with Lowest subtraction (if applicable)
- For teams: each member's individual steps plus the team sum step

Each step is expandable. Tapping it shows the full formula and the numbers used. This is the definitive answer to "why is my handicap X?" questions.

## Handicap History Charts on Your Profile

The **Handicap** tab on your profile shows a visual history of your index over time:

- A **bar chart** plots each round's handicap differential. Nine-hole round bars appear in a lighter color; 18-hole rounds in another.
- A **step-line** overlaid on the bar chart traces your calculated Handicap Index at each point in time. It steps (rather than slopes) because the index only changes at discrete round completion events.
- Tapping any bar shows a tooltip with the date, course name, total score, differential, and the index that resulted from that round.

Below the chart:
- **Lowest handicap**: Your career low index, with the date it was achieved.
- **Highest handicap**: Your career high.

The chart uses rounds from your selected date filter plus up to 40 rounds before the start of that range to ensure the index line is accurately seeded even at the left edge of the chart.

## Handicap Index Explanation Dialog (Profile Breakdown)

When your source is **Squabbit** or **League Events**, tapping your profile handicap opens a table-style explanation showing:

- A table of every round evaluated, with date, course name, total gross score, and differential
- Rounds highlighted in color are the ones **actively used** in the current calculation
- The differential formula spelled out
- How many differentials are in the pool, how many are being averaged, and any WHS adjustment applied
- Exceptional round markers (* and **) with an explanation of what they mean
- The final resulting Handicap Index

If you use **WHS/GHIN**, the dialog instead notes that the index is pulled from your linked external account and gives the date of the last successful refresh.

## Frequently Asked Questions About Handicaps

**Why is my net score different from what I calculated?**
The most likely reasons are: course handicap conversion is enabled and you are playing harder or easier tees than the neutral standard (slope 113); a percentage allowance is reducing your strokes; or the game uses Compare with Lowest so you only receive strokes relative to the best player. Tap your handicap on the scorecard for a full breakdown of every step.

**Why did my handicap go up after a good round?**
Your index is the average of your best recent differentials. If your new round's differential is not as low as the others already in your best pool, and it replaces an older very-low differential that aged out of the 20-round window, the average can rise slightly.

**My handicap says "Not Set" — what should I do?**
You need at least one completed round recorded, or you can enter a manual value from your profile. Go to your profile, tap **Handicap Index**, change the source to **Manual**, and enter a starting value.

**The app shows +2 — is that correct for a plus handicap?**
Yes. A plus sign before the number indicates a plus handicap (better than scratch). Squabbit displays +2 with a leading plus sign to match scorecard convention. Internally the value is negative, meaning you give strokes back on the easiest holes rather than receiving them.

**Why do 9-hole rounds appear differently in the explanation table?**
Two 9-hole rounds are paired together into a combined 18-hole equivalent before they count in your pool of 20. Until a 9-hole round has a partner, it sits in a waiting state. Once paired, the combined differential joins the pool like any 18-hole round.

**Can the organizer override my handicap for just one round?**
Yes. Organizers can set a per-round handicap override for any player in a multi-round tournament. This overrides the tournament handicap for that specific round only and appears as a distinct step in the playing handicap explanation.

**What happens if I have my WHS source linked but the lookup fails?**
If Squabbit cannot reach the external service, it keeps your last successfully fetched index. You will see an error message if the refresh fails, and you can tap Refresh again later or temporarily switch to Manual to enter a value.

---

# Flights, Teams, Pairings, and Tee Times

## Flights, Teams, Pairings, and Tee Times

This section covers how players are grouped for competition (flights), how teams are structured (for team formats), and how pairings, matches, and tee times are scheduled. Both player-facing actions and admin configuration are covered here.

## Flights

A **flight** is a subgroup of players (or teams) competing against each other within their own division. Tournaments often use flights so that high-handicap players compete fairly against each other instead of against scratch golfers. Each flight has its own leaderboard and its own winner.

Flights live on a **format**. Different formats in the same tournament can have different flights, or no flights at all. A flight has:

- A **name** (defaults to "Flight A", "Flight B", etc., but you can rename it freely)
- A **list of players or teams** assigned to the flight
- Optional **handicap range** (lower and upper bound)
- Optional **gender filter** (Male, Female, or Any)
- Optional **age range** (lower and upper age)
- An optional **manual winner** override (admin only)

### Individual flights vs team flights

If the format is an **individual format** (such as Stroke, Stableford, Quota, Matchplay), flights contain players. If the format is a **team format** (such as Best Ball, Scramble, Ambrose), flights contain teams. The Edit Flights page automatically switches between the two depending on the format you are editing.

### Auto-generating flights

Tournament organizers can auto-generate flights instead of building them by hand. From the format's flights page, tap the auto-generate option to open the **Auto Flights** bottom sheet.

The auto-generate settings include:

- **Clear existing flights** (when on, wipes any current flights before creating new ones; when off, new flights are added alongside existing ones)
- **Generate flights** (checkbox to actually run the algorithm vs only setting things like gender or age filters on existing flights)
- **Flight creation method** — choose how players are distributed across flights:
  - **By handicaps** (default): sorts players by handicap and splits them into equal-sized flights. The lowest-handicap players go into Flight A, the next group into Flight B, and so on.
  - **By leaderboard**: uses an existing leaderboard's standings (tournament or league) to distribute players. Useful for second-stage flighting based on qualifying scores.
- **Number of flights** — how many flights to create. Minimum 1, maximum 40. The default is 2.
- **Flight gender** — when set, all generated flights get this gender filter (Any, Male, or Female). Not available for team formats.
- **Flight age range** — optional minimum and maximum age, applied as a filter to all generated flights. Not available for team formats.
- **Auto-add new players** (handicap algorithm only) — when on, the system writes a handicap range onto each flight, computed from the midpoints between flights. New players added to the tournament after generation will automatically slot into the flight whose handicap range covers their handicap. Not available for team formats.

When you tap **Generate**, the system divides participants evenly across the requested number of flights. With the handicap method, players are sorted by handicap first, so the lowest handicaps go into Flight A. Players without a handicap on file are skipped by the handicap algorithm.

### Auto-add new players by handicap range

When **Auto-add new players** is on (handicap algorithm only), the generator writes a **lower** and **upper handicap bound** onto each flight. The bounds are computed from the midpoints between adjacent flights' handicap ranges. For example, if Flight A's high handicap is 8.0 and Flight B's low handicap is 10.0, Flight A's upper bound becomes 9.0 and Flight B's lower bound becomes 9.0.

Once these bounds are set, any time a new player joins the tournament (or has their handicap changed), they are automatically allocated to the flight whose handicap, gender, and age criteria they match. Admins do not have to remember to assign late-arriving players. If a player matches multiple flights, the first match wins.

You can also set the handicap range, gender, or age range manually on a flight by opening the flight's edit page and toggling **Use handicap range / gender / age**. The same auto-allocation runs when you change any of these filters.

### Manual flight creation and editing

From the format flights page, tap the add button to create an empty flight. The flight edit page lets you:

- **Rename the flight** by tapping its name. If all assigned players have last names available, you'll be offered a "Use last names" option that names the flight after its members (e.g., "Smith/Jones/Garcia")
- **Set handicap range, gender, and age range** (toggled on/off per flight)
- **Add or remove players or teams** — opens the entity picker, which shows all participants grouped by their current flight assignment plus an "Unassigned" section. Checking a player moves them into this flight (and removes them from any other flight automatically, so there are no duplicates across flights)
- **Search, sort, and select all** in the picker: a search box filters the list by name, a sort control orders it by name or handicap, and a **select all / none** toggle bulk-checks the visible players. Many pickers can also **copy from** another format so you reuse an existing selection instead of rebuilding it by hand
- **Delete the flight**

### Copy flights instead of rebuilding them

When you tap the add button on the format flights page, the menu offers shortcuts so you don't have to build flights from scratch:

- **Copy flights from another format**: If another format in the same tournament already has flights, copy that flight setup into this format.
