- **Copy flights from league**: On an individual format inside a **league event**, you can copy the parent league's flights straight into the event. If the event already has flights, you're warned that copying replaces them.

Both options bring over the flight structure (names, members, order). They don't copy format-specific scoring settings like points distributions.

### Handicap range conflict warnings

If you set a handicap bound that overlaps another flight's range, the app warns you that another flight covers that handicap. You can choose to continue or cancel. The warning prevents you from accidentally creating overlapping flights that would catch a new player in two places.

### Editing flight assignments mid-tournament

You can edit flights at any time, even after rounds have started. Moving a player between flights changes which flight leaderboard they appear on but does not affect their scores. This is useful when a new player joins late, a player drops out, or you decide to re-flight after qualifying.

### Manual flight winner override (admin only)

By default, the flight winner is whoever finishes first on the flight leaderboard. Admins can override this and force a different player or team to be marked as the flight winner. From the tournament's user or team dialog, an admin sees a **Set as winner** action. Tapping it stores that user or team as the flight's manual winner. The flight leaderboard then shows that entity as the winner regardless of scoring.

This is useful for resolving ties manually, applying a custom tiebreaker, awarding a winner based on factors outside the app, or correcting a scoring edge case. To clear the override, an admin opens the same dialog and taps **Remove as winner**. Formats without flights have a tournament-level manual winner override too.

### Flight leaderboards

Each flight has its own leaderboard, accessible from the format leaderboard page via a flight selector. The flight leaderboard shows only the players or teams assigned to that flight, ranked among themselves. Net and gross scoring, handicaps, points, and dropped scores all work per flight, the same way they work at the tournament level.

Importantly, FedEx-style season points are ranked **within the same flight only**. If a tournament has flights, season point allocation for that tournament is computed per flight, so winning Flight B gives the same points as winning Flight A (depending on the points table). This keeps season standings fair across mixed-skill fields.

## Teams

A **team** is a group of players who play together under one of the team formats (Best Ball, Scramble, Matchplay, Ryder Cup, Six-Six-Six, etc.). Each team has:

- A **name** (admin-editable, or auto-generated)
- A **color** (used in match cards, leaderboards, and the schedule view)
- A **set of player IDs** (non-blind plus blind)
- An optional **manual handicap** override

### Team size by format

Every team format has a minimum and maximum team size. The values depend on the scoring rules:

- Two-person formats (Alternate Shot, Chapman, Nassau, Best Ball with 2 players, Sixes, Six-Six-Six, etc.) require teams of 2
- Scramble, Ambrose, Best Ball, and Shamble allow 2 to 5+ players
- Ryder Cup has exactly 2 teams (you cannot add a third)
- Nines is a 3-player game
- Wolf is a 4-player game

When you try to add a player who would exceed the format's max team size, the app shows a "max team size exceeded" dialog. Pick a smaller team or remove a player first.

### The format catalog

Squabbit supports a wide range of team and individual formats, including Best Ball, Best Balls, Scramble, Ambrose, Shamble, Alternate Shot, Chapman, Nassau, Best Ball 1-2-3, Cha-Cha-Cha, Low Ball High Ball, Low Ball Low Total, Low Handicap High Handicap, Lone Ranger, Dots, Scotch, Vegas, Three Putt Poker, Twos Club, Fewest Putts, Skins, Team Skins, Team Sum, Team Average, Team Quota, Team Matchplay, Team Mixed, Six-Six-Six, Three-Three-Three (9-hole variant of Six-Six-Six), Ryder Cup, Worst Ball, One Gross One Net, Liv, Sixes, Nines, Wolf, Stroke, Stableford, Quota, Matchplay, Eclectic, Best Front Nine, Best Back Nine, Bingo Bango Bongo, Elimination, Team Elimination, season-point formats based on total or average scores, and more. Each has its own scoring rules; the team configuration UI adapts to each format's requirements.

### Blind players

A **blind player** is a placeholder slot on a team representing a real player whose score is "borrowed" for team scoring without that player actually being added to the team's normal roster. This is common in formats where one team is short a player and another team's player is loaned for scoring purposes only.

Blinds are available on most team formats, including Best Ball, Scramble, Shamble, Ambrose, Low Ball / High Ball, Low Ball Low Total, Low Handicap High Handicap, One Gross One Net, Team Mixed, Nassau, Scotch, and now **Ryder Cup**, **Team Matchplay**, and **Team Elimination** too.

To set a blind player, open the team edit page and choose **Add blind player**. The picker shows participants who are not already on the team (and not already blind on it). Tap one to assign them as a blind. They appear on the team with a `(B)` prefix next to their name and are stored separately from the non-blind team members.

To remove blind status, tap the player on the team and choose **Remove as blind**. This does not remove them from the tournament; it only clears their blind designation on this team.

Blind players still record their own scores on their own tee time. The team simply pulls those scores into its own scoring.

### Ghost players (filling an odd field)

A **ghost player** is a synthetic placeholder used to even out an odd field, for example an odd number of players in a Ryder Cup, or any team or match format that needs balanced sides. Unlike a blind player (which borrows a *real* player's score), a ghost is not a real person: it shoots **net par on every hole**, so it fills the slot without handing either side an unfair edge.

You add a ghost the same way you add anyone to a team, match, or tee time: use the **Add player** option and choose to add a **Ghost**. You can leave it unlinked (a generic "Ghost"), or link it to a specific absent player so it appears as "Ghost (filling in for [name])" and its scores are attributed to that player. In a league event, linking the ghost to a missing member is offered right in the add flow.

### Manual team creation

From the format's teams page, tap the add button. You'll see options depending on context:

- **Create new team** — opens an empty team edit page where you set the name, color, and add players one by one
- **Randomize teams** — auto-generation (see below)
- **Balance teams** — auto-generation (see below)
- **Copy teams from another format** — clones the team roster from a different team format in the same tournament (e.g., copy your Best Ball teams over to your Scramble format)
- **Import from another tournament** — copies teams from another tournament you administer (shown when you have other tournaments)
- **League team** — for tournaments inside a league, choose to add existing league teams to this tournament

### Auto-generating teams

Two automatic team generation methods are available:

- **Randomize teams** — shuffles all participants randomly and distributes them into teams. Useful when you want fair random pairings with no handicap bias.
- **Balance teams (by handicap)** — sorts players by handicap and pairs the best with the worst, second-best with second-worst, and so on, to create teams of roughly equal combined handicap. This requires every participant to have a handicap on file. If any are missing, the app prompts you to add them before generating.

When you auto-generate, the app first asks if you want to **remove existing teams**. After confirming, it asks for a **team size** (unless the format has a fixed size like 2-person teams, or Ryder Cup which is fixed at 2 teams). Then you pick a **naming style**:

- **Numbered** — teams are named "Team 1", "Team 2", etc.
- **Player names** — teams are named after their members' last names (e.g., "Smith/Jones")

The generator creates teams, applies colors from a default color list (with a different color per team), and saves them. You can rename or recolor any team afterward.

### Copy teams from another format

If your tournament has more than one team format and one already has teams, you can copy the entire team roster from that format into this one. The new format gets the same teams, same players, same names, same colors. This is useful when you want the same partnerships across multiple games in the same event.

### Import from a previous tournament

Admins can import teams from any tournament they administer. After picking the source tournament and (if it has more than one team format) the source format, you select which players to bring over. The teams are recreated with the same names. Any imported players who are not yet in the current tournament are added automatically.

### League teams

For tournaments inside a league that has team formats, the team-add menu offers a **League team** option. You can select existing league teams to assign to this tournament event. League teams keep the same identity across multiple tournaments in the league, so season scoring works correctly.

### Team colors

Each team has a color used throughout the app: team headers, match cards, leaderboard, schedule cards, and printed scorecards. You can change the color from the team edit page by picking from a palette. Auto-generated teams cycle through a default palette to keep colors distinct.

### Team naming options

When teams are auto-generated, you choose between numbered names ("Team 1", "Team 2") and player-name names (last names joined with slashes). After generation you can rename any team by tapping its header on the teams page or the name field on the team edit page. Flight names work the same way: you can pick "Use last names" or enter a custom name.

### Team handicap

A team's effective handicap is the sum of its members' individual handicaps, with format-specific handicap percentages applied (e.g., Best Ball commonly uses lower percentages so high handicaps don't dominate). The display value appears next to the team name in the teams list.

### Manual team handicap override (admin only)

For combined-team games where the team's total handicap matters, admins can override the auto-computed team handicap with a specific value. This is stored as the **manual handicap** on the team. Once set, the system uses that value instead of summing players' handicaps. To clear the override, edit the team and remove the value. Useful when the auto-computed handicap doesn't reflect what the field wants (such as agreed-upon adjustments for sandbaggers or for handling players without an established handicap).

### Reordering teams

On the teams page, drag the team's drag handle to reorder. This affects display order in lists and the order teams appear on schedule cards. For some pairing methods, team order also determines tee time order.

### Unassigned players section

If players are tournament participants but not on any team in the format, they appear in the **Unassigned players** section below the teams list. Tap one to add them to a team (or move them between teams).

## Match generation (matches and pairings)

A **match** is a head-to-head competition between players or teams within a tournament round. Matches are required for formats like Matchplay, Ryder Cup, Team Matchplay, Elimination, and any format where two specific teams or players are paired against each other. Some formats (like Nines) use single-team match groups that determine who plays in the same group on the course rather than head-to-head opponents.

### Auto-scheduler

From the tournament schedule view, an admin can tap the auto-scheduler action to generate matches and tee times for the round in one pass. The **Auto Scheduler** dialog has a section per format that uses matches, plus a tee times section at the bottom.

Per format, you choose:

- **Clear existing matches** (wipe the format's matches for this round before generating)
- **Generate matches** (checkbox to actually run match generation)
- **Team creation algorithm** (when applicable: only shown when format teams are larger than the match team size, e.g., a 4-person Best Ball team needs to be split into 2-person Matchplay teams for individual matches)
- **Match algorithm** (how to pair opponents)

### Match generation algorithms

The auto-scheduler offers the following match algorithms (availability depends on the format):

- **Smart Shuffle** — randomizes pairings while avoiding repeat opponents across tournament rounds (and across the whole league if the tournament is part of a league). The default option. Best when you want random but fair matchups across a multi-round event.
- **Similar Handicaps** — pairs opponents with similar handicap levels. The lowest handicap plays the second-lowest, the third-lowest plays the fourth-lowest, etc. Best when you want close, competitive matches.
- **Opposite Handicaps** — pairs the highest handicap against the lowest, the second-highest against the second-lowest, etc. Best when you want balanced match-play results because handicap strokes will tend to equalize the field.
- **Round Robin** — pairs players or teams who have not yet played each other in earlier rounds. Use in multi-round events where everyone should eventually face everyone else. Within a league, it considers prior league event rounds too.
- **Elimination** — uses an elimination bracket. Only winners from the previous round advance to play each other. Not available in the first round (because there are no winners yet), and requires at least two winners from the previous round. Use for knockout-style brackets; pairs with the Team Elimination or Elimination format types.

### Team creation algorithms (for matches)

When a format has teams larger than the match team size (e.g., a 4-person team that needs to be split into 2-person pairings each round), you also pick how teammates are chosen:

- **Smart Shuffle** — randomizes pairings within the team while avoiding repeat partnerships across rounds
- **Similar Handicaps** — teammates have similar handicaps
- **Opposite Handicaps** — pairs the highest-handicap teammate with the lowest, etc.

### Manually editing matches

The matches view (within the tournament schedule) shows every match for the round, with both teams' players and a vs separator. Admins can:

- Tap a player slot to **swap them with another player**. The picker offers all eligible players and warns if you're pulling someone from another match.
- Tap an empty player slot to **add a player** to the match
- Tap a player and choose **Choose another player** to replace them
- Tap a player and choose **Add substitute** or **Remove as substitute** (when the tournament is in a league and substitution is allowed)
- Use the overflow menu on a match to **Remove match** entirely, or **Add player** for some scoring types that allow growing the match size
- For Six-Six-Six and similar segmented formats, admins can customize the individual matchplay pairings per segment by tapping a player card inside the segment box

### "Not in a match" prompt

When you try to start a round, the app checks whether everyone is in a match for any format that requires them. If anyone is missing, you'll see a **Users not in match** dialog listing those players. From there you can jump to **Set up matches** to fix the gap, or cancel to start the round anyway.

### Locking a round (admin only)

Each tournament round has a **Lock round** toggle on the edit-round page. When locked, scorecards in that round become read-only: no one can enter or change scores, edit tee times, or modify matches for that round. Use this to freeze final results after the round is complete and you don't want late edits. Unlock the round to allow edits again.

When a tournament is marked complete, all its rounds are automatically locked. Reopening the tournament unlocks them.

## Schedule Tab Chips

Inside any tournament, the **Schedule** tab has a row of chips at the top so you can flip between different views of the round:

- **My rounds** (participants only): A big card showing your own round at a glance: your tournament round number, course, date, tee time, starting hole, the avatars of everyone in your group, and a per-nine scorecard preview. A rounded **Scorecard** button at the bottom opens your live scorecard. Non-admin participants land on **My rounds** by default so they get straight to the part that matters to them. If you have not been added to a tee time yet the chip stays available and the card shows a hint with how to get into one.
- **Tee times**: The full schedule of every group, used by admins setting up the event and by players browsing the field.
- **{Format} matches** (when a format uses matches): One chip per format that pairs players or teams head-to-head, so admins and players can review pairings before the round starts.

Switch between rounds using the round chips just below.

## Tee times

A **tee time** (also called a scorecard slot or a group) is a group of up to 8 players who tee off together at a specific time on a specific starting hole. Tee times can be auto-generated by the scheduler or created manually.

### Auto-generated tee times

In the same Auto Scheduler dialog used for matches, the tee times section lets you:

- **Clear existing tee times** (remove the round's current tee times before generating new ones; only shown if scorecards already exist)
- **Generate tee times** (checkbox to actually run generation)
- **Tee time creation algorithm** (how players are distributed across tee times)
- **Players per tee time** (group size, 1 to 8, default 4)
- **Group by flight** (when on, groups players from the same flight together with each flight going off in order; players not in any flight go last; you select which format's flights to use if more than one format has flights)

### Tee time algorithms

Available algorithms depend on the formats in the round:

- **From Matches** — creates one tee time per match, so opposing teams play together. Requires a format that uses matches.
- **Smart Shuffle (Individual)** — randomizes tee time groupings while avoiding repeat groupings across rounds (and prior league events when applicable). Best for events with multiple rounds where you want variety.
- **Team Shuffle** — randomly assigns teams to tee times. Used in team formats where teammates should tee off together. Available only when at least one format has teams of 6 or fewer.
- **Team Order** — teams go out in the order they appear on the teams page. Useful if you've manually ordered teams (e.g., by flight or seeding).
- **Similar Handicaps** — groups players with similar handicaps together. Pair with the **Tee time order** setting:
  - **Lowest handicaps first** (scratch group goes out first)
  - **Lowest handicaps last** (high handicaps go first, scratch closes the round)
- **Opposite Handicaps** — distributes handicaps so each tee time mixes low and high handicaps
- **Leaderboard Standings** — orders tee times by an existing leaderboard's standings (you choose which format's leaderboard to use). Pair with **Leaders first** or **Leaders last** to control whether the top of the standings tees off first or last. The classic "leaders go out last" is common for final-round pairings.

### Group by flight

When **Group by flight** is on, the chosen flight goes off as a contiguous block on the schedule. Within each flight, the tee time algorithm above still applies. Flights are sent off in their saved order (Flight A first, Flight B second, etc.). Players not in any flight go after all flighted groups.

### Shotgun starts

Each tournament round has an **Is shotgun start** flag. When on, instead of consecutive tee times all starting from hole 1, scorecards can start on different holes simultaneously. This is typical for charity events and large fields where everyone tees off at once.

When shotgun is enabled, the round's tee times share the same start time and you assign each scorecard a starting hole (1 through 18). Auto-generation distributes scorecards across holes. You can also set the starting hole manually per scorecard.

### Per-round shotgun group letter assignment

When multiple scorecards share the same starting hole (because the field is bigger than 18 groups), each group gets a **letter suffix** (A, B, C, etc.) so the field can tell them apart. When you set a starting hole on a scorecard via the schedule view, if other scorecards are already on that hole in shotgun mode, the app prompts you to pick a group letter. It pre-suggests the next unused letter and shows the letters already in use.

The result is groups like "Hole 5, Group A", "Hole 5, Group B", "Hole 5, Group C", which appear on printed scorecards, the schedule view, and in player notifications.

### Tee time intervals

For non-shotgun rounds, tee times are spaced at a configurable interval (default 8 minutes). The interval lives on the tournament round settings. The scheduler uses it to determine the next available time slot when adding new tee times.

### Manually creating tee times

From the schedule view, admins can tap the floating button and choose **Create empty tee time** to add a slot without running the auto-scheduler. The new tee time is added at the next available time. Then tap the empty slot to add players.

### Editing tee times

From a tee time card, admins (and players when the tournament settings allow) can:

- Tap a player to change their tee, transport, or marker, or to remove them
- Tap an empty slot to add a player from the participant list
- Use the overflow menu to: add player, edit starting hole, edit date (multi-day rounds), edit time, change course (multi-course rounds), or remove the tee time
- Tap the time to change the start time
- Tap the starting hole to change which hole the group tees off on (and choose the shotgun group letter if applicable)
- Tap the course to switch between courses (when the round has multiple)
- For a player on a tee time, **Edit tee** changes which tee box they're playing from for that round only. Useful for guests on different tees from the rest of their group, or when a player wants to move up or back. Requires the round to have multiple tees available on the course.

When removing a player from a tee time who has already entered scores, the app asks whether to **Keep scores** (their scores stay in the system; only the tee time slot is freed) or **Delete scores** (full removal).

### Self-select tee times settings

The tournament has three player-facing tee time permissions in the tournament settings, configurable by admins:

- **Allow self tee time selection** — when on, participants can choose their own tee time slot (or remove themselves from one). Defaults to on.
- **Allow others tee time selection** — when on, a player who is in a tee time can edit who else is in that same tee time (add friends, change peers). Defaults to on.
- **Allow tee time creation** — when on, players can create new empty tee times themselves (otherwise only admins can). Defaults to off.

These three combine. Admins always have full control, regardless of these toggles. The toggles only relax the rules for non-admin participants. When all three are off, only admins can manage tee times.

### Per-player tee assignment

Inside a tee time, admins can change a player's tee box independently of the rest of the group. Tap the player, choose **Change tee**, and pick from the round's available tees on that course. The player's round records this tee, and their stroke index and yardages reflect it. The rest of the group keeps their own tees.

This is also how you handle mixed gender groups where players use different tees, or guests playing from a different tee than the regular field. Players with the right permissions can change their own tee.

### Multiple courses in one round

A tournament round can use more than one course. When the round has multiple courses, each scorecard is assigned to a specific course (visible in the tee time card header). Admins can change a scorecard's course via the overflow menu. Players can change their own scorecard's course when **Allow player course selection** is enabled in the tournament settings.

### Course Source: Presets vs Any Course

When **Allow player course selection** is on, a **Course source** sub-tile appears underneath it. This decides what list of courses a player can pick from:

- **From presets**: Players can only choose from the courses an admin has already added to the tournament's rounds. Good for events where you want everyone playing one of a handful of approved layouts.
- **Any course**: Players can search Squabbit's full course database and even add a brand-new course on the fly. Good for casual leagues where members are playing wherever they like.

Admins are not restricted by this setting — they can always swap to any course. The toggle only governs what non-admin players see in the picker.

When you change a scorecard's course, the system also picks tees automatically for each player based on their preferred tee (if matched on the new course) or the round's selected tees for that course.

### Auto-add teammates to tee times

When a player creates a tee time and joins it, the app automatically adds their teammates to the same tee time (up to 5 of them, when they don't already have a tee time). This makes setup easier when teams are using player-self-scheduling. For elimination-bracket formats, the player's match opponent is also auto-added if they don't already have a tee time.

## Substitutions

A **substitute** (sub) is a replacement player added to a tournament in place of a participant who can't make it. Subs only exist in **league tournaments** (not standalone tournaments).

### Who can add a sub

- **Admins** can always add a sub for any participant. This is the default flow.
- **Players** can add a sub for themselves only when the tournament's **Allow player match substitution** setting is on (off by default). With it on, a non-admin can open their own profile in the tournament, tap **Add substitute**, and pick from eligible league members. Players can only sub for themselves — they cannot initiate a sub for another player.

Use the toggle when you trust your field to manage their own absences. Leave it off when you want every substitution to go through an admin.

A player who is already a sub themselves cannot be subbed for. A player who already has someone subbing for them cannot be subbed a second time.

### Adding a sub

When you tap to add a sub for a player:

1. The app opens a picker showing **league participants** and **league spectators** who are not already in the tournament. Sysadmins are filtered out.
2. Pick the substitute. The selected user is added to the tournament as a participant, with their **subbing for** field pointing to the original player.
3. The original player is moved to **Spectator** status so they no longer count as an active participant.
4. The sub is added to the original player's scorecards across every round.
5. The sub replaces the original player in every **match**, **flight**, and **team roster** across all formats. Their strokes flow into the original player's team total in Best Ball and other team formats automatically.

The sub's name appears with an `(S)` prefix in team and match displays so it's clear they're a substitute.

### Removing a sub

To remove a sub (admins or the sub themselves can do this):

1. The original player is restored in every match and flight the sub was filling.
2. The sub is removed from the tournament entirely (along with their scorecards).
3. The original player is restored to Participant status.

Note that any scores the sub entered before being removed are lost when the sub is deleted. If you need to preserve their work, leave the sub in place; don't remove them.

### Ghost players in subs

If the substituted-out player was linked to a ghost (a placeholder player) on their teams, the ghost transfers to the sub automatically so team rosters stay intact.

## Tying it together: admin workflow checklist

A typical tournament setup uses these features in this order:

1. **Add players** to the tournament
2. **Configure formats** (one or more scoring formats per round)
3. **Build teams** (for team formats) using auto-balance, randomize, copy from another format, or manual creation
4. **Build flights** (for any format that needs them) using auto-generate by handicap, or manually
5. **Set tee time permissions** in the tournament settings (allow self-selection, creation, others)
6. **Run the auto-scheduler** per round to generate matches and tee times in one pass
7. **Review and adjust** matches and tee times manually as needed
8. **Lock the round** when scoring is final, or mark the tournament complete to lock everything

Players see what they're allowed to see at each step. They can self-select tee times if enabled, see their matches once generated, and view their flight leaderboard when results come in. Admin overrides (manual flight winners, manual team handicaps, round locks) exist throughout to handle every real-world edge case.

---

# Score Entry and Stats

## Score Entry and Stats

This section covers how players and organizers enter scores in Squabbit, the different tools available for score entry, how stats are collected during a round, and where you go to review your stats after the round.

## Score Entry

### The Scorecard Screen

When you open a scorecard, you see a grid of all players and holes. Squabbit supports two layout orientations:

- **Portrait mode**: Holes run down the rows, players across the columns.
- **Landscape mode**: Players run down the rows, holes across the columns.

The app automatically picks a layout based on your device orientation. To lock a preferred orientation, open the scorecard's overflow menu, tap the gear icon to open **Scorecard settings**, and use the **Scorecard orientation** toggle. The setting is saved on your account and follows you across every scorecard.

At the top of the scorecard, a summary header shows each player's running total and their score relative to par. Tap the small arrow icon to collapse or expand it. Game chips beneath the header let you switch between the different formats and side games active on the scorecard (for example, Gross, Net Strokeplay, Stableford, or any side game you added).

A live **leaderboard** is also available from the bottom navigation. Every time a score is saved it updates immediately so everyone watching can track standings in real time.

### Entering a Score Hole by Hole

Tap any cell in the scorecard grid to open the score entry dialog for that hole. The dialog shows the hole number and the player's name. A grid of score buttons runs from 1 through 11, plus a ">11" button for higher scores. Tapping a score number saves it immediately. No "Done" button is needed unless the dialog is also collecting stats or game-specific information (see below).

Scores use standard golf visual notation in the dialog and on the scorecard:

- **Eagle or better**: Solid green circle
- **Birdie**: Open circle
- **Par**: No decoration
- **Bogey**: Open square
- **Double bogey**: Solid black square
- Scores worse than double bogey escalate in visual weight (darker squares)

If you use a physical Bluetooth keyboard, you can type the score directly and the dialog closes automatically.

### Scores Above 11

Tap the ">11" button in the score grid. A number entry dialog appears where you can type any score from 1 to 25.

### Pick Up

When the Pick Up option is enabled (it appears in certain game formats), you can record that a player picked up their ball and did not finish the hole. Pick Up is stored internally and does not add to the player's stroke total in the same way a normal score does. For scoring purposes (for example, Stableford or when a max hole score cap is applied), Squabbit substitutes the max allowable score for the hole.

### Entering a Total Score Only

If you only have a final total (for example, after a round where you did not track individual holes), you can enter just the total score from the scorecard overview. A round is considered complete when either all holes have scores entered, or a total score greater than 10 is saved with no hole-by-hole data.

### Scorecard Settings (Personal Preferences)

The scorecard's overflow menu (three dots, top right) includes a **gear icon** that opens the **Scorecard settings** page. The values you choose here are saved on your account and follow you to every scorecard you open.

- **Auto-advance**: Controls what happens after you save a hole score. Options include **Next player** (move to the next player on the same hole), **Next hole**, **Off**, and a few others — each option has its own description in the dialog. Pick the flow that matches how you score.
- **Unit of measurement**: Toggle between **yards** and **meters**. Affects distances on the scorecard, the map shot-tracker, and shot distance dialogs.
- **Scorecard orientation**: Toggle between **Portrait** and **Landscape** if you prefer to lock the layout rather than let the device orientation decide.

### Per-Player Options on the Scorecard

Tap your name or avatar on your scorecard row to open a per-player options sheet. From here you can:

- **Change tee**: Switch which tee you're playing from for this round.
- **Count towards handicap** (toggle): On by default. When off, this round is excluded from your Squabbit-calculated Handicap Index but still saves with all your scores and stats. Use it for practice rounds, scrambles, or any round you don't want affecting your handicap.
- **Remove from scorecard**: Take yourself off this scorecard. Any scores you've entered are kept on a round attached to your profile.

The options visible depend on your role (player vs. admin vs. marker) and what the tournament settings allow.

### Editing Past Scores

Completed scorecards can be reopened and edited subject to the permission rules described below. Open the old scorecard, tap any cell, and the same entry dialog appears. Changes are saved immediately.

### Entering Hole Competition Distances (Closest to the Pin, Longest Drive)

When you save a score on a hole that has a distance competition (closest to the pin, longest drive, straightest drive, or a custom contest), Squabbit asks "This hole has a {competition} competition. Would you like to add a new entry to the leaderboard?" Entries can also be added from the competition's card in the leaderboard's **Games** view. After picking the player, you choose how to capture the distance:

- **Measure with camera**: Measure the distance with your phone's camera using augmented reality.
- **Enter manually**: Type the distance in.

**Measuring with the camera** works on iPhones and Android phones that support AR:

- On phones with a LiDAR sensor (recent iPhone Pro models), **Point** mode shows a crosshair over the live camera view: stand at the ball, point the crosshair at the pin, and the live distance appears. Tap **Use this distance** to accept it.
- Every AR-capable phone also has **Walk** mode: aim at your ball on the ground and tap **Mark ball**, then walk to the pin, aim at its base, and tap **Mark pin**. The straight-line distance between the two marks is calculated. Tap **Redo** to measure again or **Use this distance** to accept.

The measured value pre-fills the distance field in the entry dialog, so you can still review or adjust it before saving. Closest-to-the-pin entries use inches or centimeters; longest drive entries use yards or meters, following your unit preference. On phones without AR support you go straight to manual entry.

## Hole Stats: Detailed Per-Hole Data

The score entry dialog has a **Show Stats** toggle at the bottom. Tapping it expands a full stats section so you can record detailed data alongside the score.

Depending on the tournament or round configuration, some stats fields may be **required** (the dialog will not close until you fill them in). Required fields appear above the score grid; all others appear in the expandable Stats section.

Stats you can record per hole:

- **Putts**: Tap 0, 1, 2, 3, or "4+" (which opens a number input). When 2 or more putts are recorded, a **putt miss direction** row appears — tap left, long, short, or right to indicate where the ball missed.
- **FIR (Fairway in Regulation)**: Tap the check mark (hit) or X (missed). On a miss, tap a direction arrow to record where the tee shot went. The FIR field only appears on par-4 and par-5 holes.
- **GIR (Green in Regulation)**: Tap the check mark or X. On a miss, tap a direction arrow. When you have both a score and a putt count entered, GIR is inferred automatically — the app calculates whether you could have reached the green in regulation based on the score minus putts.
- **Bunker shots**: Tap 1, 2, or 3.
- **Chip-ins**: Toggle yes or no. Recording a chip-in (one or more chip shots and zero putts) sets the chip-in flag that feeds into game events such as Chippie.
- **Lost balls**: Tap 0, 1, 2, or 3. Lost-ball totals roll up into your stats charts alongside putts, GIR, and FIR, so you can see whether errant tee shots are costing you strokes over time.
- **Penalty areas**: Tap 1, 2, or 3.
- **Chips** (number of chip shots around the green): Tap 1, 2, or 3.
- **Mulligans**: When the scorecard has mulligans enabled, a row shows how many you have remaining and lets you add or subtract with +/- buttons.

### Clearing a Hole

Inside the score entry dialog, a **Clear hole score** button appears whenever a score or any stats have been entered. Tapping it removes the score and all stats for that hole and immediately saves the change.

## Max Hole Score (Auto-Applied Cap)

Tournament organizers can configure a **maximum hole score** rule per format (tournament edit page > Setup tab > Formats > tap the format > Max hole score). When a player's score exceeds the cap, the excess is disregarded for that format's scoring calculations. You do not need to adjust scores manually: the raw entered value stays on the scorecard and the cap is applied during scoring only, so a format with a more lenient cap (or no cap) on the same tournament still sees the full score.

The cap options are:

- **Gross double bogey / gross triple bogey / gross quadruple bogey**: An absolute cap regardless of handicap (for example a par 4 with a gross double bogey cap means the max counted score is 6).
- **Net double bogey**: The cap equals the hole par plus the strokes the player receives on that hole, plus two more strokes.
- **Net double par**: The cap scales proportionally with par and the player's strokes given.

When a player records a Pick Up, the max hole score value is substituted for that hole in all game calculations.

## Marker and Admin Score Entry

### Who Can Enter Scores

For casual (non-tournament) rounds, any player on the scorecard and the person who created it can enter or edit scores.

For tournament rounds, the following restrictions apply:

- **Only admins can enter scores** enabled: Only tournament admins can tap cells and save scores. Players see the scorecard as read-only.
- **Tournament round locked**: Nobody can make changes.
- **Allow scorecard entry any time disabled**: Players can only enter scores on the same calendar day as the round date.
- **Prevent edits after completion enabled**: A scorecard becomes read-only once all holes are filled and you navigate away.

### Marker System (Score Attestation)

Tournaments can enable a **Markers** feature, which lets one player (the marker) independently record scores for another player. This mirrors how formal golf competitions handle scorecards.

How markers are assigned:

- **Admin-assigned**: Tournament admins assign markers to players before the round.
- **Player-selectable**: Players can choose their own marker from other participants in the tournament.

When a marker enters scores for a player, those scores become the official record. The player being marked can also independently enter their own scores, which are stored separately as **unofficial scores**.

When the round is complete, the marker sees a **Submit Scores** button. Tapping it opens a side-by-side comparison of the marker's scores and the player's unofficial scores, hole by hole. Holes where the two differ are highlighted in red. The marker taps **Submit** to officially finalize the scores.

## Score from Image (AI Scorecard Import)

Squabbit can read scores from a photo of a filled-in printed scorecard using AI. This is a **Host Pro** feature: if the group doesn't have an active Host Pro unlock, the Host Pro screen appears first (see the Host Pro and Purchases section in the settings guide).

### How to use it

1. From an open scorecard, tap the overflow menu (three dots, top right) and select **Import Scores from Camera**.
2. A confirmation dialog appears explaining what the feature will do. Tap **Continue**.
3. Your photo gallery opens. Select a photo of the scorecard.
4. Squabbit sends the image to a cloud AI service for parsing. A loading indicator appears while this runs.
5. A **preview dialog** appears with the scores the AI detected, laid out as a scorecard table. Scores use the same visual notation as the main scorecard (circles for birdies, squares for bogeys, etc.) along with each player's total.
6. Tap any score cell to correct a number the AI misread.
7. Tap **Confirm** to apply the scores, or **Cancel** to discard them.

### Tips for a good photo

- Lay the scorecard flat on a well-lit surface.
- Ensure all hole scores and player names are clearly legible.
- Avoid shadows, glare, or cut-off edges.

### Player matching

After you confirm, Squabbit matches the player names detected in the image to the players on the current scorecard. It tries exact name matching, shortened-name matching, and first-name matching (when the first name is unique on the scorecard). If a name cannot be matched automatically, you are asked to either **Skip** that player or **Select** the correct player from a list.

## Round Version History

Squabbit keeps a hidden history of every set of hole scores ever saved on a round. If a round's scores are wrong (someone tapped the wrong cell, an admin edited the wrong row, the AI import overwrote good data), you can roll the round back to an earlier snapshot.

### How to restore an earlier version

1. Open the scorecard and tap the overflow menu (three dots, top right).
2. Tap **Version history**.
3. Pick the player whose round you want to restore.
4. A list of historical snapshots appears, each with a timestamp and the full hole-by-hole scores. Pick the version you want.
5. A **Replace round scores** confirmation shows the current scores side by side with the historical scores. Tap **Replace** to overwrite the round with the older version, or **Cancel** to back out.

The player's handicap differential is recalculated automatically after the restore so their Squabbit Index reflects the corrected scores.

Version history is different from **Recently Deleted**: deleting a round removes the whole round and lets you bring it back; version history keeps the round in place and lets you swap which hole scores it shows.

## Clear Scorecard (Admin)

Tournament admins have a destructive shortcut for wiping every hole score on a scorecard. It is useful when test scores were entered or the wrong scorecard was filled in entirely.

1. Open the scorecard and tap the overflow menu (three dots, top right).
2. Tap **Clear scorecard** (backspace icon).
3. A red confirmation dialog warns that scores cannot be undone. Tap **Clear** to proceed.

Every round attached to the scorecard has all its hole scores erased. Player rounds, the scorecard itself, and any team or flight assignments stay intact — only the hole scores are removed. This is different from deleting the scorecard, which removes everything.

Because the action is destructive, prefer **Version history** above when you only need to roll back a single player.

## Import Tournament Rounds from a Spreadsheet (Admin)

Admins can retroactively populate a tournament's scores by uploading a spreadsheet. This is useful for events that were scored on paper, or for migrating results from another scoring app.

From the tournament's edit page, open the **Other** section and tap **Import tournament rounds**. You will be guided through uploading a CSV of scores and matching its rows to the tournament's players and rounds. After import, the leaderboard recalculates automatically.

This is distinct from the **CSV import** on the Players tab (which adds players, not scores).

## Entering Scores Without an Account (Guest Scoring)

Players can now enter scores without creating an account. There are two ways in:

- **Scan the group's QR code or open its invite link** for a tournament. They're asked whether they're playing or spectating; choosing "I'm playing" starts the identity-claim flow.
- **Scan a printed scorecard or cart-sign QR code**, which drops them straight onto that scorecard.

This is a change from before: score entry used to require a printed scorecard QR, and the FAQ used to say only one player per group needed to sign up. Now **any participant can enter their own scores as a guest**.

### Claiming Your Identity

A guest enters their **name** (first and last). Squabbit fuzzy-matches it against the field, handling nicknames (Mike/Michael) and small typos. If one player matches, they confirm with an **"Is this you?"** prompt; multiple matches show a picker. Once confirmed, the identity is saved on the device and reused on later visits.

If the matched player already has a Squabbit account, the guest is asked to log in to it instead. If every player on a scorecard already has an account, a guest is told to log in or have a playing partner enter their scores.

Once identified, a guest can enter scores whenever the round's normal score-entry permissions allow it (not locked, not admin-only, within the entry window). Guest state survives app restarts; when the guest later creates a real account, their guest identity is automatically cleared.

## HoleEvents: Per-Hole Achievement Points

**HoleEvents** are custom achievements attached to certain game formats (Dots, Trash, Scotch, and others). They award or deduct points on a hole-by-hole basis as players make or miss certain outcomes.

### How they appear during score entry

When you open the score entry dialog on a hole that has active HoleEvents, toggleable events appear as switch tiles inside the dialog. You flip the switch to claim the achievement. Events that Squabbit can calculate automatically from your score and stats (birdies, greenies, one-putts, etc.) do not show toggles — they are applied automatically when you save.

### Automatic HoleEvents (determined from scores and stats)

These require the corresponding stat or score to be entered but do not need manual input:

- **Birdie or better, Eagle or better, Par or better, Par, Birdie, Bogey, Double Bogey, Triple Bogey, Quadruple Bogey, Quintuple Bogey**: Determined from net or gross score-to-par.
- **Hole in One**: Score of 1.
- **Greenie**: Green in regulation (GIR = yes).
- **Sandie**: Par or better after hitting a bunker shot (requires bunker shot count).
- **Chippie**: Chip-in (chip count recorded and zero putts).
- **One Putt, Three Putt, Four Putt**: Determined from putt count.
- **Par Scramble**: Par or better without GIR.

### Manually claimed HoleEvents (toggle in the dialog)

- **Closest to the Pin in Regulation**
- **Longest Drive**
- **Barkie** (up and down after hitting a tree)
- **Custom** events defined by the organizer with any name and point value

### Points

Each event has a point value set by the organizer. The built-in defaults are:

- Positive achievements (birdie, greenie, sandie, one-putt, etc.): **+1 point**
- Negative outcomes (three-putt, double bogey, etc.): **-1 point**

Organizers can customize values and decide whether more than one player can claim the same event on the same hole.

## Live Scoring and the Leaderboard

Every saved hole score triggers an immediate leaderboard update. In tournament rounds, the full tournament summary leaderboard recalculates as well. Anyone viewing the tournament leaderboard (on their own device or on a shared display) sees the updated standings without needing to refresh.

## Shot Tracking

Squabbit includes a GPS-based shot tracking feature that records individual shots during a round. Shot data enriches the stats available after the round and can pre-populate your score entry when tracked shots fall within a reasonable range (birdie to triple bogey relative to par). Shot tracking is a deep feature with its own dedicated experience. Stats derived from it are described below.

## Plays-Like Distance and Club Recommendation

On the scorecard's **map view**, Squabbit can show a **plays-like** panel: it takes the raw distance to your target and adjusts it for the conditions, then recommends a club from your bag.

### Turning it on

- Tap the white **golf-club button** on the map's right-hand button rail to show or hide the panel. The button turns green while the panel is on, and your choice is remembered for next time.
- The panel appears automatically once your target is within range (roughly your driver distance), and you can tap the map to set a target anywhere.
- If you have no clubs in your bag yet, the panel shows the plays-like distance with an **Edit bag** prompt instead of a recommended club. Tap the recommended club (or the distance) to open your bag.

### What it adjusts for

The panel shows the recommended club, the **plays-like distance** ("plays 168 yd"), and a row of factor cards. Each card shows how many yards that factor adds (warm color, plays longer) or removes (cool color, plays shorter):

- **Elevation**: uphill plays longer, downhill plays shorter, using the course's elevation data. Shows a dash when the course has no elevation data.
- **Wind**: a headwind plays longer, a tailwind plays shorter, from live weather. A pure crosswind doesn't change the distance but shows an **aim** hint ("aim 4 yd left") when it's strong enough.
- **Air**: thin air (hot, humid, or high altitude) lets the ball fly farther, so the shot plays shorter; dense (cold, low) air plays longer. From live temperature, humidity, and altitude.
- **Lie**: a rough or buried lie plays longer. Tap the **Lie** card to pick your current lie from a list; the recommendation updates instantly.

Tap any factor card to open a short explanation plus the exact arithmetic behind that card's yardage. Wind and air factors show a dash until live weather finishes loading.

### Where it's blocked

During an **official event** (a tournament where the organizer turned on official-event mode), distance and elevation aids aren't allowed, so the button shows a small lock badge and the panel can't be turned on. Tapping it explains why.

### Wind units

The wind card shows speed in mph or km/h based on your **Wind unit** preference. See the settings FAQ for where to change it.

## Courses: Creating and Editing

Courses in Squabbit are **shared**: every player and tournament that uses a course reads the same course record, so an edit benefits (or affects) everyone.

### The Course Editor

Open a course's page (from a scorecard, round setup, or course search) and choose **Edit course** from the menu. Because courses are shared, a warning titled **"This course is shared"** explains that editing changes the course details for everyone, not just you; tap **Edit for everyone** to continue.

The editor shows:

- A **photo banner** at the top.
- The **course name** and **location** (tap to edit).
- A **Simulator course** switch.
- A **tee selector** where you add, edit, or delete tees. Each tee stores its name, per-hole yardages, and rating and slope.
- One scorecard-style card per nine with rows for **Par**, **S.I.** (stroke index), and yardage. Tap any cell to edit it. Par and stroke index edits can update all tees at once, and the editor auto-advances to the next empty cell so filling in a card is fast.

On 18-hole courses, each tee can also store a separate **Front 9** and **Back 9** rating and slope alongside the full-18 values; these are used when a round plays only that nine. On standard 18-hole courses, the stroke index dialog also supports an optional **second stroke index** (19-36) used by Australian courses (see the Handicaps guide).

### Course Photos

You can add a photo of the course to the top of its page: in edit mode, tap **Add photo** on the banner, or use the edit and delete buttons on an existing photo. The photo appears on the course page for everyone.

### Scan a Paper Scorecard

Instead of typing a course in by hand, photograph its paper scorecard and let AI read it:

1. When creating a new course you're offered **Scan a scorecard (beta)** (or enter it manually). On an existing course, choose **Scan scorecard (beta)** from the editor menu to update it.
2. Take or choose one or more photos of the scorecard. Squabbit reads the tees, yardages, pars, and stroke indexes (and rating and slope when they're printed on the card).
3. A **preview** shows everything that was read. Map each scanned tee to a new tee or an existing one (or skip it), tap any cell to fix a misread value, then tap **Confirm** to save.

### Multi-Nine Courses (27, 36, 45, or 54 Holes)

Courses with more than 18 holes are stored with all their holes, organized into **named nines** (for example "Lakes," "Meadows," "Pines"). Each nine has its own name and its own rating and slope per tee. When you play the course, in a casual round or a tournament round, a picker asks which nines you're playing and in what order, and you can pick the same nine twice.

### Choosing Holes to Play (Casual Rounds)

When starting a casual round from the **Play** tab:

- On an 18-hole course, choose **Full 18**, **Front 9**, or **Back 9**. Full 18 also lets you pick a starting hole.
- On a 9-hole course, play it once or loop it twice as an 18-hole round.
- On a multi-nine course, pick the nines to play and their order.

Tournament rounds can additionally combine nines from two different courses into one 18-hole layout (see the setup guide).

## Stats Squabbit Tracks

### Where Stats Live

Your personal stats are on your **Profile** page, under the **Stats** tab. Chips across the top let you switch between stat categories. Stats are calculated from your completed rounds in the selected date range.

### Date Range Filter

A filter chip at the top right shows the active date range and how many rounds are included. Tap it to choose from:

- **6 Months**
- **Year to Date**
- **1 Year** (the default)
- **2 Years**
- **3 Years**
- **All Time**

Pull down to refresh the stats screen at any time.

## Stat Categories

### Handicap

The Handicap tab shows two things:

1. **A chart of your handicap history**: Bar columns show each round's differential (9-hole rounds in a different color from 18-hole rounds). A step line traces your handicap index as it changed after each submission. Tap any bar for a tooltip showing the date, course, handicap at that time, total score, and differential.
2. **Lowest and highest handicap** you have held in the selected period, each with the date it occurred.

### Scoring

The Scoring tab shows:

- **Round scores chart**: A bar chart with one bar per round showing your total score. Tap a bar for a tooltip with date, course, and score.
- **Lowest 18-hole score** and **highest 18-hole score** in the selected period, each with the course and date.
- Per-round averages (normalized to 18 holes) for: **eagles**, **birdies**, **pars**, **bogeys**, **double bogeys**, **triple bogeys or worse**.
- Average strokes per hole by **par 3**, **par 4**, and **par 5**.
- Total **hole-in-ones**.
- Longest streaks: **birdie-or-better**, **bogey-free**, **double-bogey-free**, **triple-bogey-free**.

### Driving

The Driving tab combines hole-level FIR data with shot-tracker driving data (the latter requires shot tracking to be active during rounds):

- **Average drive distance** (shot tracking required)
- **Longest drive** with course, hole number, and date — tap to see a list of your top 50 drives
- **Average fairways in regulation** percentage
- **Highest and lowest fairway % in a single round**
- **Fairway miss direction** breakdown: percentage missed left vs right
- **Longest consecutive FIR streak**

### Approach

The Approach tab uses GIR data from per-hole stats and approach proximity from shot tracking:

- **Dispersion plot**: A scatter diagram showing where your approach shots landed relative to the pin, color-coded by starting distance bucket (50-100 yd, 100-150 yd, 150-200 yd, and 200+ yd). Tap any dot for the hole number, proximity, club, course, and date.
- **Average greens in regulation** percentage
- **Most and fewest GIRs in a single round**
- **Green miss direction**: percentage missed left, long, short, right
- **GIR by par level**: separate percentages for par 3, par 4, and par 5 holes
- **Longest consecutive GIR streak**
- **Average proximity to the pin** by distance bucket and by club (for clubs with enough tracked approach shots)

### Short Game

The Short Game tab covers shots inside 50 yards from the pin that ended near the green:

- **Dispersion plot** for chips (0-15 yd), pitches (15-30 yd), and partial wedges (30-50 yd), showing where shots landed relative to the hole
- **Up and down percentage**: How often you made par or better after missing the green (requires shot tracking or GIR stat)
- **Sand save percentage**: How often you made par or better from a bunker (requires shot tracking)

### Putting

The Putting tab is one of the most detailed areas, drawing on the putt count and putt miss direction you enter per hole plus tracked putt data:

- **Dispersion plot** of missed putts by distance bucket (3-5 ft, 5-10 ft, 10-20 ft, 20+ ft), showing where the ball ended up relative to the hole. Tap any dot for hole and round details.
- **Average putts per round** (normalized to 18 holes)
- **Average putts per hole**
- **Putt miss direction** breakdown: left, long, short, right
- **Longest 3-putt-free streak**
- **Putting percentage by putt count**: 0-putt %, 1-putt %, 2-putt %, 3-putt %, 4+-putt %
- **Fewest and most putts** on the front 9, back 9, and full 18 in any single round
- **Make % by distance**: How often you converted putts from 3-5 ft, 5-10 ft, 10-20 ft, and 20+ ft

### Bag

The Bag tab shows your median tracked distance for each club, sorted from driver to putter. Median is used rather than average so that a handful of unusual shots do not distort the typical number.

- If you have set up a bag in your profile, every club appears. Clubs without enough tracked shots show a dash as a placeholder.
- If you have not set up a bag, only clubs with at least 3 tracked shots appear.

## League-Level Stats

When you play in a league, Squabbit aggregates your stats across every completed tournament event in that league. League stats are available on the league's own stats screen (not your personal profile) and include:

- Score distributions: hole-in-ones, eagles, birdies, pars, bogeys, double bogeys, triple-or-worse, in both gross and net
- FIR and GIR counts
- Total skins won
- Leaderboard placement history (wins, top-3 finishes)
- Total purse winnings

League stats are recalculated each time an event in the league is finalized.

---

# Leaderboards and Output

## Leaderboards Overview

Leaderboards are reached from the **Leaderboard** tab on a tournament or league. They show live results as scores come in, organized into a stack of chips that let you drill down without leaving the tab.

### Chip rows

At the top of every tournament leaderboard you will see a row of **format chips**. There is one chip per format (for example "Net Best Ball", "Gross Stroke Play", "Skins"). Tap a chip to switch which format you are viewing. If the tournament also has hole competitions, side games, purses, or game stats enabled, extra chips for **Games** and **Stats** appear at the end.

Below the format chips is a second row of chips for that format:

- **Overall**: the cumulative result across all rounds in this format. This is the default view.
- **Round 1**, **Round 2**, etc.: the leaderboard for a single round only. Round chips switch to the per-hole hole-by-hole view, where every hole shows that player's score with par-relative coloring and a "Thru" column tracks holes completed.
- **Bracket**: only appears for Elimination or Team Elimination formats; shows the knockout bracket instead of a table.

Under the round chips is a third row of **flight chips**. If the format has flights, you can tap a flight name to filter the leaderboard to just that flight. If any players are not assigned to a flight, an **Unassigned** chip appears so the admin can see who still needs to be placed.

### Columns on the overall view

The overall leaderboard is a card-style table with two locked columns on the left (position and player or team) and a horizontally scrollable area on the right that holds:

- **Pos**: the player's place. Ties are shown as "T3" and so on. If the player is tied but resolved by a countback, a small star appears next to the position. Tap the star to open a dialog explaining exactly which tie-breaker rule decided the order. Players who did not finish show **DNF**, and missed-cut players show **CUT**.
- **Player / Team**: avatar (unless turned off), name, and optionally handicap. Tap the name to open the player actions dialog.
- **R1**, **R2**, etc.: that round's total in this format. Tap a cell to jump into the round's scorecard. If the player has not started yet, the cell shows a **play icon** along with their tee time so you can launch the round directly from the leaderboard.
- **$ column** (currency symbol): only appears when the format has a purse attached. Shows each player's or team's winnings in the format's currency.
- **Tot**: the total to date in this format. For team rows the total appears in a brand-green color. If the player or team has any manual score modifiers (see below) the modifier total appears as a small secondary line under the total ("+5" or "-2") so it is obvious the total is not just hole scores.

The **Tot** column header has a sort/filter icon. Tap it to open a settings dialog where you can:

- Sort the overall leaderboard by a specific round instead of by cumulative total.
- Switch your **score display preference** between total strokes and score-to-par. This preference is saved per scoring type (stroke play has its own, Stableford has its own, etc.) and follows you across every tournament.

### Cut line

If the tournament format has a cut configured, a red **CUT** divider is drawn between the players who made the cut and those who missed. Players below the line show **CUT** in place of a position.

Cut lines are configured per format on the format edit page, under the **Cut line** section. Tap **Add cut line** to create a new rule. Each cut rule has:

- **Cut after round**: Which round triggers the cut. In a multi-round event you can configure several cuts (after round 1, after round 2, etc.) so the field narrows progressively. If the format only has one applicable round, the picker is skipped.
- **Cut position**: The top **N** players who advance. The rest are marked CUT.

Tap an existing cut rule to edit or remove it. Players who miss the cut still appear on the leaderboard with **CUT** in their position cell — they just stop accumulating scores against the field going forward.

### Team grouping

When the format is a team format, every row that represents a team is followed by an indented list of that team's members. A small triangle next to the team name lets you collapse or expand all members at once. Each member row shows their individual round scores even though their position is determined by the team total. A colored vertical stripe on the left of every team row matches the team color so you can scan the leaderboard at a glance.

### Hole-by-hole round view

Tapping a round chip switches to the hole-by-hole view for that round. Each row is a player or team and each column is a hole. Hole scores are color-coded relative to par (eagles, birdies, pars, bogeys, etc.). Hole-competition holes are tinted in the column header so players know which holes count for the closest-to-the-pin, longest drive, or custom prizes. Tap any cell to open the scorecard at that hole.

### Bracket view

For knockout formats the **Bracket** chip shows the full matchup tree with connectors between rounds. Admin taps on a match cell let you mark the winner manually if scores cannot decide it. The format's **Bracket layout** setting controls how the tree is drawn: **Left to right**, or **Out to in** where the two halves of the draw start on the outer edges and converge on the final in the center.

### Score display preference

Some games can be shown as a raw total ("75") or as a score relative to par ("+3"). Open the **Tot** column settings to flip your preference for stroke play, Stableford, and other supported scoring types. The choice is stored on your user account, not on the tournament, so once you set it you will see it everywhere.

### Avatars and handicaps in the leaderboard

Two tournament-level toggles control what the player column looks like:

- **Show handicaps in leaderboard**: when on, each player's handicap is rendered under their name.
- **Show avatars in leaderboard**: when off, the avatar circles are hidden, leaving a denser text-only layout.

Both toggles live in the tournament's **Edit Tournament** page and apply to all viewers. Leagues have their own **Show handicaps in leaderboard** toggle on **Edit League**.

### My Round banner

On the overall leaderboard, the very top of the table shows a small **My Round** banner if you have a round you can start or are in the middle of playing. The banner is a shortcut: tap it to open your live scorecard for the relevant round without scrolling down through the leaderboard to find yourself. It hides itself once your rounds are complete or if you are only spectating.

### Net vs gross variants

Squabbit treats net and gross as separate formats. If the tournament admin sets up both **Net Stroke Play** and **Gross Stroke Play**, they show up as two distinct format chips. Each format has its own purse, its own flights, its own modifiers, and its own winner. Players see the **Net** label or **Tot** label in the column header on the scorecard PDF depending on whether the chosen format uses handicaps.

### Games chip

When a tournament has hole competitions, vs-style side games (matchplay segments, Nassau, etc.), or any format-level purse, an extra **Games** chip appears at the top of the leaderboard tab. The Games view groups results by section:

- **Distance competitions**: a card per closest-to-the-pin, longest drive, or straightest drive contest showing the current leader and their distance. Tap a card to see the full list of submitted distances, add entries, and edit them. When adding an entry you can type the distance or **measure it with your phone's camera** using augmented reality (see the Score Entry guide for the measuring flow).
- **Matchplay results**: one card per side-game match showing the two players or teams, their scores, and the running result ("3 up thru 12", "All Square", etc.).
- **Total winnings**: a card per player or team summarizing how much they have won across all purses in the tournament so far.

Within the Games view a top row of round chips lets you flip between **Overall** (all rounds combined), **Round 1**, **Round 2**, and so on, so you can see who won each round's side games independently.

### Stats chip

If **Show stats** is on for the tournament (the default), a **Stats** chip joins the chip row. The Stats view ranks players across the field on metrics like fairways in regulation, greens in regulation, putts per round, scrambling percentage, etc. Negative stats can be hidden if the admin turns on **Hide negative stats** (so a missed-fairway leaderboard, for example, becomes a fairways-hit leaderboard). Stats need scorecards with the matching tracking enabled (the admin can require putts, GIR, FIR, and lost-ball tracking from **Edit Tournament**) for the columns to be meaningful.

In some tournaments the detailed stats view (per-hole breakdowns, scoring trends, and the AI recap) requires a one-time **Unlock stats** purchase; see the Host Pro and Purchases section in the settings guide.

### AI tournament recap

At the top of the tournament's **Stats** view, Squabbit can generate a written **tournament recap**: an AI summary covering the final standings, scoring swings across rounds, hole-competition winners, and notable stats.

- The recap generates automatically as the final round wraps up (once roughly 90% of the field has finished scoring), and it can be generated or regenerated manually with the **Regenerate** button (organizers, or in stats-unlocked tournaments the viewers who unlocked stats).
- When generating, a **Choose a style** sheet offers two tones: **Banter** (a sports recap with friendly ribbing) or **Professional** (a clean tournament report). When the tournament has **Hide negative stats** turned on, only Professional is offered.
- The recap is saved on the tournament, so every player and spectator sees the same text.
- Use **Copy** to copy the text to your clipboard or **Share** to send it anywhere. Regenerating overwrites the saved recap for everyone.

### Tie-break star and dialog

When two or more players or teams have the same raw total, the position cell shows "T3" (or whatever tied place applies). If Squabbit had to apply a configured tie-breaker (countback, lowest last-9, lowest last-3, lowest stroke-index hole, etc.) to pick a definite order, a small star appears next to the position. Tap the star to open the **Tie break details** dialog, which lists every step of the tie-break logic that ran and exactly which hole or value broke the tie. This is helpful for explaining to a player why they finished 4th instead of T3.

## Manual Score Modifiers (admin)

Squabbit has a powerful admin feature for putting a thumb on the scale: **score modifiers**. They let you add or subtract points from a player's or team's leaderboard total without changing any hole scores. This is how you give a team a "head start", award best-dressed points, dock someone for being late, or move a tied player ahead. Modifiers apply on top of the regular hole scoring and they are scoped to the specific format you are viewing, so adding +5 in your Net Best Ball format does not affect Gross Stroke Play. The same feature exists on the league leaderboard, where modifiers apply to the cumulative season-long total.

### Modify user score

To adjust a single player's score:

1. Go to the **Leaderboard** tab and select the format you want to modify.
2. Tap the player's **name** in the leaderboard.
3. In the dialog that opens, tap **Modify user score**. (This option only appears for admins, and not on team formats; for team formats use the team modifier instead.)
4. A list of existing modifier entries appears. The first time it will be empty.
5. Tap **Add entry**.
6. Enter a **reason** (for example "Best dressed", "Late penalty", "Head start") and a **points** value. The points can be positive or negative and can include decimals. Tap **Add**.
7. Repeat to stack multiple entries. The total of all entries is what gets added to the player's leaderboard total.
8. Tap **Save** to apply.

Once a modifier is saved, the player's row on the leaderboard shows the modifier total as a small secondary line under their **Tot** value (for example "+5" under "287"). The current modifier total also shows as a subtitle under **Modify user score** when you reopen the dialog, so you do not need to open the entries list to see whether a modifier is in effect.

### Modify team score

For team formats, modifiers are applied to the **team** rather than to any individual member:

1. Make sure you are on a team format chip.
2. Tap the **team name** in the leaderboard (the colored bold text).
3. A team actions dialog opens. Tap **Modify team score**.
4. The entry list works the same as for users: tap **Add entry**, enter a reason and a points value, save. Add as many entries as you need.

The team's row total is adjusted by the sum of the team's modifier entries. Individual members do not see the modifier on their own totals; only the team total moves.

### Viewing, editing, and removing modifiers

Open the **Modify user score** (or **Modify team score**) dialog again at any time to see the full list of entries. Each entry shows its reason and its signed points value ("+5" or "-2"). To **edit** an existing entry, tap it; you can change the reason or the points and save. To **delete** an entry, tap the trash icon on its row. To clear the modifier entirely, delete every entry and save. The leaderboard updates as soon as you save.

### Why use modifiers instead of editing scores

Modifiers are visible, auditable, and reversible. They keep the actual hole-by-hole scores untouched (so handicapping, stats, and per-round leaderboards are still accurate) and they show up as a clearly-labeled secondary line on the leaderboard. They are the right tool for non-stroke adjustments like skill challenges, costumes, side bets, or league-wide bonus points.

### League modifiers

The same feature works on the league leaderboard. From the **Leaderboard** tab on the league, tap a player's name or a team's name and choose **Modify user score** or **Modify team score**. League modifiers are per-format and per-season, so they apply to the cumulative season total but do not change any tournament's internal scoring.

## Manual Winner Overrides (admin)

Squabbit picks winners automatically using the format's tie-break rules. Sometimes you need to overrule that, for example to honor a coin-flip or a chip-off that happened off-app.

### Manual flight winner

For individual stroke-play-style formats with flights but no tie-break method configured, admins can mark a specific player as the **flight winner**:

1. Open the **Leaderboard** tab and select the relevant flight chip.
2. Tap the player's name.
3. Choose **Set as winner**. The player is now treated as the flight winner regardless of scores.
4. To remove the override later, tap the same player's name again and choose **Remove as winner**.

### Manual overall winner

For formats with no flights, the same **Set as winner** action on a player's name marks them as the overall format winner. The same flow handles removal via **Remove as winner**.

Manual winner overrides are stored per format, so designating a winner in one format does not affect any other format running in the same tournament.

### Manually overriding a tie

When Squabbit resolves a tie with the configured tie-breaker, the position cell shows a star. Tap it to open the **Tie break details** dialog. On individual formats, an admin sees two extra actions there:

- **Override manually**: Opens a **Pick the winner** chooser. Selecting a player applies a pairwise override that decides the tie in their favor (the dialog then reads "Manually decided in favor of {name}"). Use this for a coin-flip or chip-off that broke the tie off-app.
- **Remove override**: Clears any manual tie override and lets the automatic tie-breaker decide again.

## Hiding the Leaderboard

Tournament admins can hide live results during play. From **Edit Tournament**, turn on **Hide leaderboard results**. While hidden, players still enter their own scores but cannot see anyone else's totals or rankings. The leaderboard tab shows an "eye-off" icon and a short message explaining that an admin has hidden results.

Three sub-options let you soften the lockdown:

- **Hide after hole**: enter a hole number (1 to 18) for the final round. Up to and including that hole players can still see live results; after they have passed that hole their own scores become hidden too. This is useful for a sealed-leaderboard finish over the last few holes.
- **Show for admins**: when on, admins still see the full leaderboard even though it is hidden from players. This lets the organizer monitor the field in real time.
- **Show when my rounds are complete**: when on, a player who has finished all their rounds gets to see the leaderboard, so they can watch the rest of the field come in.

A player can always see their own scores. The hide rule applies only to other players' totals, so the **My round** banner at the top of the leaderboard still shows your own progress.

Games and Stats chips are hidden along with the leaderboard, since those views would reveal results indirectly.

## Auto-Scrolling Leaderboard

For projecting the leaderboard on a clubhouse TV or tablet, Squabbit ships an **Auto-scrolling leaderboard** mode. From **Edit Tournament**, tap **Auto-scrolling leaderboard**. You will see the same format and flight chips as the regular leaderboard, plus a **play/pause** button in the app bar. This is a **Host Pro** feature (see the Host Pro and Purchases section in the settings guide).

When you tap play, Squabbit:

1. Selects the first format and first flight.
2. Slowly auto-scrolls from the top to the bottom of the leaderboard.
3. Pauses briefly at the bottom, jumps to the next flight, and scrolls again.
4. After cycling through all flights for that format, advances to the next format and repeats.
5. Loops indefinitely.

Tap pause to stop, or tap any chip to manually jump to a specific view. Auto-scroll speed adapts to the number of rows so a short flight does not blow by too quickly.

## Lock Screen Live Leaderboard

While you're playing a tournament round, Squabbit can show the live leaderboard right on your **lock screen** so you can glance at it without unlocking and opening the app. On iPhone this is a **Live Activity** (lock screen and Dynamic Island); on Android it's an **ongoing notification**.

### How it works

- The card starts automatically when a tournament round you're in goes live, and it ends when the round finishes.
- It updates automatically as scores come in, showing the top rows plus your own row when you're outside the top.
- Each row shows position, name, score, and how many holes a player is through. Your row is highlighted.
- For **Ryder Cup**, the card emphasizes the projected match score (e.g. "USA 8½ vs EUR 7½") drawn from confirmed and projected points.
- When the tournament has more than one format, the card can page between them.
- Tap the card to open the tournament in the app.

### Dismissing it

Swipe the card away to dismiss it, and it won't come back for that round. A future round of the same tournament can start a fresh card. The feature only appears on devices whose OS supports it.

## League Leaderboards

The league leaderboard tab works like the tournament one but represents the cumulative season standings across every event in the league.

### Columns

- **Pos**: place in the league.
- **Player / Team**: avatar and name. Tap to open the same modifier dialog described above.
- **Events**: appears only if **Show num events** is turned on in **Edit League**. Shows how many league events each player has completed.
- **$ column**: appears only when the league has a purse and **Show winnings** is on.
- **Total**: the league-format total. If the player has not yet reached the league's **Min events to rank**, the total is rendered in red and tapping it shows a "X of N events completed to rank" hint.
